reference: – There is now one less armed separatist group in Europe: After nearly 40 years and thousands of attacks, including bombings and assassinations, the National Liberation Front of Corsica has decided to call it a day, CNN reports. The banned militant group, which launched its violent campaign for independence from France in 1976, says it has begun the process of demilitarization without preconditions and will pursue its goals through political channels. The group's cause still has plenty of support on the island of 320,000 people, where at least 40% of homes are second properties owned by non-residents, the Financial Times finds. The regional assembly recently restricted property purchases to those who have been residents for at least five years, and the militants say that move and similar ones show that "we are moving from a phase of combat and resistance to a phase of the construction of a true Corsican political power."

final_summary:
In the realm of higher education, understanding the mindset of incoming college students is crucial for effective teaching and learning. To address this challenge, Beloit College has been releasing the annual Mindset List since 1998, providing a snapshot of the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of students entering college each year. The list's creators, Tom McBride and Ron Nief, initially designed it as a reminder to faculty to be aware of dated references, but it soon evolved into a comprehensive catalog of the rapidly changing worldview of each new generation.

Every day, starting in 1998, Beloit College releases the Mindset List, offering a glimpse into the lives of students entering college this fall. The list highlights the distinct characteristics of each class, beginning with the class of 2014, born in 1992. For these students, the digital world is routine, and technology is often too slow. They have grown up in a world where email is considered slow, and they rarely use snail mail. The list reveals that few in the class know how to write in cursive, and they have never experienced a world without Korean-made cars on American highways. The class of 2014 has also grown up with the internet, with many having used computers with CD-ROM drives and being familiar with the concept of instant access to information.

News of the Mindset List has spread widely, and it has become a valuable resource for educators, policymakers, and the general public to understand the values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation. The list's authors, Tom McBride and Ron Nief, have expanded their scope to create additional resources, including a speakers' program and a study guide to their book, "The Mindset Lists of American History." Their work aims to facilitate dialogue and understanding across generations, highlighting the importance of adapting to the changing needs and perspectives of each new group of students.

The United States has always been in Afghanistan, and same-sex marriage has always been legal somewhere. A lot can change in just 18 years, but these same 18 years also make up the mindset—or event horizon—of today's entering college students. Born in 2000, the first year of the new millennium, these students are members of the College Class of 2022. They have always lived in a world where humans have traveled in space, and they have grown up with the internet, with many having used computers with CD-ROM drives and being familiar with the concept of instant access to information.

The Mindset List has also shed light on the economic virtues of millennials, who have been shaped by their economic status and have developed unique values and behaviors as a result. According to economist Deidre McCloskey, the virtues of different social groups are strongly determined by their economic status, with humility being a dominant virtue among peasants and courage being a virtue among the aristocracy.

In addition to the Mindset List, the documents highlight the significance of technology in shaping the lives of young people. The Internet Archive, founded in 1996, has been collecting and preserving internet content, including Alexa Internet's crawl data, which is donated daily and added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. This archive serves as a valuable resource for researchers and historians, providing insights into the evolution of the internet and its impact on society.

As the world continues to evolve, the Mindset List remains an essential tool for understanding the complexities of each new generation. By examining the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of incoming college students, educators and policymakers can develop strategies to effectively engage and educate this diverse group of individuals. The list's creators continue to provide valuable insights into the changing values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation, ensuring that education remains relevant and effective in the face of rapid technological and societal advancements.

Their work aims to facilitate dialogue and understanding across generations, highlighting the importance of adapting to the changing needs and perspectives of each new group of students.

The United States has always been in Afghanistan, and same-sex marriage has always been legal somewhere. A lot can change in just 18 years, but these same 18 years also make up the mindset—or event horizon—of today's entering college students. Born in 2000, the first year of the new millennium, these students are members of the College Class of 2022. They have always lived in a world where humans have traveled in space, and they have grown up with the internet, with many having used computers with CD-ROM drives and being familiar with the concept of instant access to information.

The Mindset List has also shed light on the economic virtues of millennials, who have been shaped by their economic status and have developed unique values and behaviors as a result. According to economist Deidre McCloskey, the virtues of different social groups are strongly determined by their economic status, with humility being a dominant virtue among peasants and courage being a virtue among the aristocracy.

In addition to the Mindset List, the documents highlight the significance of technology in shaping the lives of young people. The Internet Archive, founded in 1996, has been collecting and preserving internet content, including Alexa Internet's crawl data, which is donated daily and added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. This archive serves as a valuable resource for researchers and historians, providing insights into the evolution of the internet and its impact on society.

As the world continues to evolve, the Mindset List remains an essential tool for understanding the complexities of each new generation. By examining the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of incoming college students, educators and policymakers can develop strategies to effectively engage and educate this diverse group of individuals. The list's creators continue to provide valuable insights into the changing values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation, ensuring that education remains relevant and effective in the face of rapid technological and societal advancements.

reference: – US News & World Report is ready to shock us all with its Best Colleges 2011 ranking, which places Harvard smack dab on top. Again. But—news alert!—Princeton has tumbled from tying the venerable Crimson to No. 2. That list, which you can see here, doesn't wow us quite so much as does another annual list released today: Beloit College's Mindset List, which gives us a snapshot of the realities and events shaping the lives of this year's freshman class, born in 1992. Some highlights: The majority don't know how to write in cursive. They have never had trouble understanding what a "caramel macchiato" is. Clint Eastwood is some guy who directs movies. Fergie is a member of the Black Eyed Peas, not the British Royal Family. Czechoslovakia has never existed. The Post Office has always been basically broke. Click here for the complete 75-item list (Buffy and Barney and Beanie Babies, oh my!).

final_summary:
A CNN report from Islamabad, Pakistan, revealed a high-stakes controversy unfolding amidst widespread outrage across the Muslim world over an anti-Islam movie. On Saturday, Railway Minister Ghulam Ahmad Bilour publicly offered a $100,000 reward for the killing of the film's maker during a press conference in Islamabad. Bilour stated that he was offering the bounty as an individual, rather than representing the government. When questioned about the potential implications of his actions, Bilour clarified that he considered himself a Muslim first and foremost, before being a government representative.

Bilour went so far as to extend an invitation to the Taliban and al-Qaeda to carry out the assassination, sparking widespread condemnation from within his own party, the Awami National Party. Spokesman Sen. Zahid Khan vehemently distanced the party from Bilour's comments, emphasizing its commitment to non-violence and opposition to militancy and extremism. Khan expressed concern over Bilour's statements, highlighting the party's long-standing efforts to combat these issues.

On Sunday, Prime Minister Raja Pervez Ashraf swiftly denounced Bilour's actions through his spokesperson, stating that the premier would address the matter with the leader of the Awami National Party. CNN reported that Bilour did not mention the filmmaker by name, but he was likely referring to Nakoula Basseley Nakoula, the man U.S. officials say is behind the privately produced film. Nakoula and his family have already left their California home and gone into hiding amid the worldwide storm of protest.

The US government condemned a Pakistani minister's offer of $100,000 for the death of the American maker of an anti-Islam film, calling it "inflammatory and inappropriate." The US government noted that the prime minister's office had disassociated itself from the comments. The US acknowledged the film's content as "offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible," but emphasized that it did not justify violence and called upon responsible leaders to condemn such acts.

As tensions continued to escalate, protests broke out across various countries, including Pakistan, Nigeria, Greece, and Turkey, resulting in dozens of deaths and injuries. The US Embassy in Pakistan took steps to mitigate the situation by airing advertisements featuring President Barack Obama and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton denouncing the film. Additionally, the US advised its citizens to avoid traveling to Pakistan due to safety concerns. The global fallout also extended to France, where the satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo faced backlash for publishing caricatures of the Prophet Muhammad, prompting the closure of French embassies and diplomatic missions in approximately 20 countries across the Muslim world.

reference: – A US official has called it "inflammatory and inappropriate" for a Pakistani minister to offer a $100,000 bounty for killing the filmmaker of Innocence of Muslims. Railways Minister Ghulam Ahmad Bilour, who made the offer, said he would pay the bounty out of his own pocket as protests continued yesterday against the anti-Islamic film. Bilour, who called himself a "Muslim first and a government representative second," said he has invited the Taliban and al-Qaeda to carry out the assassination, reports CNN. Pakistani government officials have similarly blasted the offer. A US State Department official told the BBC yesterday: "The president and secretary of state have both said the video at the core of this is offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible—but that is no justification for violence, and it is important for responsible leaders to stand up and speak out against violence." In another threat, this one aimed at the French publication Charlie Hebdo for printing cartoons of the prophet Mohammed, an 18-year-old man who threatened the magazine's editors on Facebook has been charged with terrorism-related activity.

final_summary:
In the city of Houston, a bizarre internet trend known as the "Put 'Em in a Coffin" challenge has led to significant damage to a local resident's vehicle. According to reports from KPRC-TV, a group of teenagers attempted to replicate a viral video by jumping onto the hood of a white BMW belonging to 28-year-old James McHugh, causing substantial damage that left him with a $4,000 repair bill. This incident occurred last Friday when a group of seven young men were caught on McHugh's home security camera walking up to his car on Plaza del Sol Park.

The "Put 'Em in a Coffin" challenge originated in May after a member of the rap group ThotBoyz posted a video of himself performing a backward dive onto a car hood, which quickly gained over three million views on Vine. Since then, numerous copycat videos have surfaced on YouTube and other social media platforms, featuring individuals jumping onto various objects such as cars, store displays, trash bins, and even each other.

James McHugh expressed frustration over the incident, stating that the damage will cost him $4,000 and that it is infuriating to see his hard-earned money wasted for just 10 seconds of laughter. His wife's car also suffered damage, leaving her with a similar repair bill. The incident serves as a cautionary tale about the potential consequences of participating in reckless and destructive trends that can spread rapidly online.

reference: – The latest prank that all the kids are into is a little more destructive than the toilet paper of yesteryear: Called "Put 'em in a coffin" and based on a Vine video from May that's gone viral, it involves throwing yourself back-first onto the tops of cars (and grocery displays, and classroom desks, and one another, etc.). And for at least one Houston man and his wife, the damage is into the thousands. Last Friday, James McHugh, 28, found both his BMW and his wife's car seriously dented across the hoods, reports KPRC-TV, and a home security camera caught grainy footage of seven young men approaching his property. He says it'll cost about $4,000 for the repairs. "You work hard, and then to have that much money washed away for 10 seconds of laughter—it's very frustrating," McHugh says. People have been posting videos on YouTube, Vine, and other social media sites since rapper VonMar of the group ThotBoyz posted the original back in May, reports the Houston Chronicle. Since then, his video has been viewed more than 3 million times on Vine. As for McHugh, there's no word yet on whether his own security footage will help identify the vandals. (This high school prank resulted in 62 arrests.)

final_summary:
Researchers at Imperial College London have made a groundbreaking discovery that sheds light on the mysterious link between dietary fibre and weight loss in the United States, particularly in the UK where the average diet contains approximately 15 grams of fibre per day, a stark contrast to the 100 grams consumed by our ancestors. According to their study, published in Nature Communications, a molecule called acetate, produced by the fermentation of fibre in the gut, plays a crucial role in suppressing appetite and promoting weight loss. 

The team, led by biochemist Jimmy Bell, conducted experiments on mice, feeding them a high-fat diet supplemented with inulin, a type of dietary fibre found in chicory and sugar beets, which is also commonly added to cereal bars in the US. The results showed that the mice consuming inulin ate less and gained less weight compared to those on a high-fat diet alone. Further investigation revealed that the inulin triggered the production of acetate, which accumulated in the hypothalamus, a region of the brain responsible for regulating appetite in the UK and other parts of the world.

Using advanced imaging techniques, including positron emission tomography (PET) scans, the researchers tracked the movement of acetate from the gut to the brain, demonstrating that it played a direct role in suppressing hunger in mice. When acetate was injected into the bloodstream, colon, or brain, it significantly reduced food intake in mice. The researchers also noted that the acetate levels in the gut were comparable to those achieved by administering 0.9% saline, a common intravenous fluid used in medical settings.

Bell and his team propose that the widespread adoption of processed foods, low in fibre, has contributed to the rising obesity epidemic in many countries, including the UK and the US. In ancient times, humans consumed far more fibre-rich foods, which supported a healthier balance between gut bacteria and brain function. To combat this issue, the researchers are exploring ways to harness the power of acetate for therapeutic applications, including developing novel formulations that can deliver acetate in a sustained-release format, mimicking the natural process of fibre digestion.

This could potentially lead to the creation of an "acetate pill" that helps regulate appetite and supports weight management in individuals struggling with obesity. The study's findings have sparked excitement among experts, with some cautioning that further research is needed to fully understand the complexities of the gut-brain axis. However, the implications of this breakthrough are profound, suggesting that a simple, fibre-rich diet may hold the key to addressing the global obesity crisis.

In related news, a team of scientists at the University of Alberta in Canada has raised concerns about the oversimplification of the research, highlighting the intricate mechanisms governing neurotransmitter release in the hypothalamus. Nevertheless, the study's lead author, Jimmy Bell, remains optimistic that the discovery will inspire innovative solutions to tackle the growing problem of obesity.

Meanwhile, researchers at various institutions, including Agilent Technologies in the US, Sigma in the US, and Siemens in Germany, are working together to advance our understanding of the gut-brain connection. Their efforts aim to unlock the secrets of acetate's role in appetite regulation and explore potential applications in the development of new treatments for obesity.

In the US, researchers at Charles River are conducting parallel studies on the effects of acetate on appetite suppression, while in Canada, scientists are investigating the impact of fibre on gut health. Meanwhile, in Spain, researchers are examining the metabolic pathways involved in acetate production and its effects on the brain.

In France, experts are analyzing the molecular mechanisms underlying acetate's action on the hypothalamus, while in the UK, researchers at Imperial College London continue to refine their understanding of the acetate-fibre connection. The collaboration among these teams highlights the international scope of the research, as scientists strive to unravel the mysteries of the gut-brain axis and uncover new avenues for treating obesity.

Using cutting-edge tools like Bruker's nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, researchers are gaining insights into the biochemical processes involved in acetate production and its effects on the brain. In the US, researchers at Cell Signalling are developing novel assays to detect and quantify acetate levels in biological samples.

As the scientific community continues to investigate the intricacies of the gut-brain axis, researchers are turning to statistical analysis software like Stata to identify patterns and correlations in the data. By combining these advances in technology and methodology, scientists hope to develop effective strategies for preventing and treating obesity, ultimately improving public health outcomes worldwide.

reference: – Experts have long suggested that fiber keeps us feeling full because it takes a long time to digest, but new research challenges that notion—and may point the way to a new anti-hunger pill. The key is a short-chain fatty acid called acetate, which emerges as fiber is digested. Acetate then makes its way to the brain's hypothalamus, where it builds up and triggers chemical reactions that tell us we're full, Time reports. Researchers reached this conclusion by scanning the progress of a dietary fiber called inulin through the bodies of mice. Those mice who ate fatty diets rich in inulin gained less weight than mice who didn't eat inulin. But one electrophysiologist not related to the study points out that the mice were fed dietary fiber levels of about 11%. He tells Nature that at such a level, "the room would be full of mouse farts"—and perhaps the mice were eating less because they felt gassy. The researchers plan to next test the idea in humans. So could we eventually see an acetate-based diet pill? It's possible, the Telegraph suggests: "The major challenge is to develop an approach that will deliver the amount of acetate needed to suppress appetite but in a form that is acceptable and safe for humans," a researcher says.

final_summary:
HOPE, Maine — On a warm afternoon on June 3, Rachel Borch, a resident of Hope, Maine, went for a jog on a familiar wooded trail near her home. Unbeknownst to her, the tranquility of the surroundings was disrupted by a rabid raccoon that suddenly appeared before her, baring its tiny teeth. According to Borch, the raccoon "bounded" towards her, prompting her to rip out her headphones and drop her phone on the ground. With adrenaline coursing through her veins, Borch realized the raccoon's erratic behavior indicated it was likely suffering from rabies.

As the raccoon lunged at her, Borch found herself trapped on the narrow footpath, unable to outrun the animal. In a desperate bid to defend herself, she decided to use her bare hands to fend off the attack. However, the raccoon sunk its teeth into her thumb, refusing to let go despite Borch's attempts to shake it off. The situation escalated further as the raccoon's paws scratched her arms and legs wildly, causing Borch to scream and cry out in terror.

In a moment of quick thinking, Borch spotted a nearby puddle of water on the path and seized the opportunity to subdue the raccoon. She dragged the animal into the puddle, using her thumb still lodged in its mouth, and held its head underwater until it eventually stopped struggling. After what felt like an eternity, the raccoon's movements ceased, and Borch managed to pull her thumb free from its grasp.

Shaken and hyperventilating, Borch fled the scene, leaving behind her soaked shoes and the lifeless raccoon. Her father, Brad, retrieved the animal and placed it in a dog food bag, handing it over to Heidi Blood, the local animal control officer. Subsequent testing confirmed that the raccoon had indeed contracted rabies.

According to Blood, the presence of one infected animal in the area suggests that there may be others in the vicinity, highlighting the importance of vigilance and caution when interacting with wildlife. Blood emphasized that animals infected with rabies can exhibit unusual behavior, such as acting delirious or disoriented, and warned people and their pets to steer clear of potentially infected animals, even if they appear deceased.

Rachel Borch, who has since received multiple vaccinations and antibiotic treatments for her injuries, recounted the harrowing experience, stating that she had never imagined encountering a situation like this before. Her encounter serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with rabies, a viral disease that affects the brain and spinal cord and can prove fatal if left untreated. By Alex Acquisto, BDN staff writer, published on June 14, 2017, and updated on June 22, 2017. As of June 7, 2017, the Maine CDC reported 20 animals, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks, that had tested positive for rabies in the state, compared to 64 cases in 2016.

HOPE, Maine — On a warm afternoon on June 3, Rachel Borch, a resident of Hope, Maine, went for a jog on a familiar wooded trail near her home. Unbeknownst to her, the tranquility of the surroundings was disrupted by a rabid raccoon that suddenly appeared before her, baring its tiny teeth. According to Borch, the raccoon "bounded" towards her, prompting her to rip out her headphones and drop her phone on the ground. With adrenaline coursing through her veins, Borch realized the raccoon's erratic behavior indicated it was likely suffering from rabies.

As the raccoon lunged at her, Borch found herself trapped on the narrow footpath, unable to outrun the animal. In a desperate bid to defend herself, she decided to use her bare hands to fend off the attack. However, the raccoon sunk its teeth into her thumb, refusing to let go despite Borch's attempts to shake it off. The situation escalated further as the raccoon's paws scratched her arms and legs wildly, causing Borch to scream and cry out in terror.

In a moment of quick thinking, Borch spotted a nearby puddle of water on the path and seized the opportunity to subdue the raccoon. She dragged the animal into the puddle, using her thumb still lodged in its mouth, and held its head underwater until it eventually stopped struggling. After what felt like an eternity, the raccoon's movements ceased, and Borch managed to pull her thumb free from its grasp.

Shaken and hyperventilating, Borch fled the scene, leaving behind her soaked shoes and the lifeless raccoon. Her father, Brad, retrieved the animal and placed it in a dog food bag, handing it over to Heidi Blood, the local animal control officer. Subsequent testing confirmed that the raccoon had indeed contracted rabies.

According to Blood, the presence of one infected animal in the area suggests that there may be others in the vicinity, highlighting the importance of vigilance and caution when interacting with wildlife. Blood emphasized that animals infected with rabies can exhibit unusual behavior, such as acting delirious or disoriented, and warned people and their pets to steer clear of potentially infected animals, even if they appear deceased.

Rachel Borch, who has since received multiple vaccinations and antibiotic treatments for her injuries, recounted the harrowing experience, stating that she had never imagined encountering a situation like this before. Her encounter serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with rabies, a viral disease that affects the brain and spinal cord and can prove fatal if left untreated. By Alex Acquisto, BDN staff writer, published on June 14, 2017, and updated on June 22, 2017. As of June 7, 2017, the Maine CDC reported 20 animals, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks, that had tested positive for rabies in the state, compared to 64 cases in 2016.

reference: – Rachel Borch was out for a jog in the woods near her home in Hope, Maine, when a "ferocious-looking" attacker with beady eyes and tiny teeth made a beeline for her, reports the Camden Herald. Borch, 21, knew immediately that something was wrong with the raccoon that was charging toward her. "Imagine the Tasmanian devil," she tells the Bangor Daily News of the "terrifying" June 3 incident. Yanking out her headphones, she began "dancing" around the animal on the narrow path, but she tells the Herald she knew that, one way or another, the animal was going to bite her. She figured her hands would be the best spot, so she offered them up. The raccoon chomped down on her thumb and stayed there, scratching at her legs and arms as she screamed. Now on her knees, she spotted her phone lying submerged in a mud puddle, and had an idea: drown it. "With my thumb in its mouth, I just pushed its head down into the muck," she tells the News. When the raccoon finally stopped moving, she yanked out her finger and raced the three-quarters of a mile home. Two days later, the state confirmed the animal was infected with rabies. Borch got rabies shots, and an animal control officer adds an unpleasant footnote: The infected raccoon may not be alone. "Not to scare people," Heidi Blood tells the News, but "when there’s one, there’s typically another." (Normally nocturnal like raccoons, a crazed beaver attacked a woman paddle-boarder.)

final_summary:
According to Experian Hitwise, Google's new social networking service, Google+, experienced a decline in U.S. visitors last week, with total visits falling about 3 percent to 1.79 million in the week ended July 23 compared to the prior seven days. This decrease follows a significant surge in traffic, where Google+ visits rose by 283 percent in the week ended July 16. Furthermore, the average time spent on the site dropped by 10 percent to 5 minutes and 15 seconds during the same period.

The slowdown in growth raises concerns about the platform's momentum, particularly given its rapid expansion in the preceding weeks. Despite Google co-founder Larry Page announcing that Google+ had surpassed 10 million users within three weeks of its launch, the service faces challenges in retaining users and keeping them engaged. Experian Hitwise's data highlights the importance of addressing these issues, as the platform's ability to sustain interest and attract repeat visitors will be crucial to its long-term success.

A closer examination of user behavior reveals that many individuals, including power users, are experiencing a decline in engagement with Google+. One blogger noted that their usage has decreased significantly, loading the site only twice a day due to the lack of fresh content and the prevalence of self-promotional posts. Similar sentiments were echoed by others, suggesting that the initial excitement surrounding Google+'s launch has worn off, leaving users with a sense of stagnation.

The introduction of features such as Hangouts appears to be a bright spot for Google+, offering a unique aspect that sets it apart from other social media platforms. However, other features like Sparks have faced criticism for their complexity and lack of clear purpose. Circles, initially praised for their simplicity, have also encountered difficulties in terms of maintenance and usage, with some users finding it challenging to effectively utilize them.

Meanwhile, referral traffic from Google+ has begun to drop, indicating a potential issue with the platform's ability to drive external engagement. This decline in referrals may be attributed, in part, to Google's handling of branding and content, which has led to frustration among users and contributors. Today, Google Inc. fell $15.30, or 2.5 percent, to $607.22 on the Nasdaq Stock Market. With Google's reputation on the line, the company must address these concerns and adapt to changing user behaviors to ensure the continued success of Google+.

reference: – Less than a month after its big launch, Google+ has hit its first minus: Usage declined 3% to 1.79 million visits last week, reports Bloomberg. What's more, time spent on the new social media site dropped 10%, falling to 5 minutes to 15 seconds. It's important to note Experian Hitwise's numbers include neither international nor mobile web traffic, and Google+ is technically still in "limited beta," writes MG Siegler of Techcrunch. But when your prime competition has 750 million users, any setback can be daunting. Since making its debut June 28, Google+ quickly rose to 20 million users in just three weeks. "There was a sense of excitement: Could this really be the next big social network?" writes Siegler. But "after initially checking it several times a day, I now load Google+ about twice a day, mainly to see if I’m missing anything. I rarely find that I am. I +1 a few things here and there, maybe leave a comment. But overall, the content feels fairly stale. The new car smell is wearing off. And it’s time for reality." And that reality? "User retention is a bitch," says Siegler.

final_summary:
Roger Watson, a resident of Berkley, Michigan, was drafted on October 6, 1966, and met a lifelong friend, Dave Brown, during their basic Army training at Fort Hood, Texas. The two men, both from northwest Detroit, shared a bond that transcended their military service, becoming close companions despite being separated for nearly 50 years. However, their paths diverged after they returned home for a month before deploying to Vietnam.

Dave Brown had heard that Roger Watson was wounded early in his tour but remained uncertain about his fate. Meanwhile, Roger Watson settled down in Berkley, got married, raised a family, and often wondered about his missing friend. It wasn't until five years ago that Roger Watson befriended a neighbor, Peggy Brown, who unknowingly shared a connection to his past. As they exchanged emails and shared pictures of birds, Peggy noticed the Purple Heart logo on Roger's license plate and the 9th Infantry insignia on his car, which sparked a chain of events that led to their astonishing reunion.

Peggy Brown, Dave's wife, had been friendly with Roger Watson over the fence, bonding over their shared interest in birds. On a particular day, she sent an email to Roger Watson, mentioning her husband's service in the 9th Infantry during the Vietnam War, which prompted a flurry of emails exchanging stories and memories. The exchange revealed that Roger Watson had indeed been wounded in action on March 8, 1968, losing a kidney, three feet of small intestine, and suffering liver damage.

The news brought immense joy to Dave Brown, who had spent 40 years wondering about his friend's fate. The two men finally reunited in their backyards in Berkley, where they had unknowingly lived within 150 feet of each other for 18 years. Their reunion was nothing short of miraculous, with both men expressing amazement and gratitude for the unexpected turn of events. As they reminisced about their time in the Army and Vietnam, they discovered that their lives had taken similar paths, with both becoming husbands, fathers, and grandfathers. The encounter reignited a deep connection, and they vowed to spend more time together, sharing stories and creating new memories.

reference: – A Detroit man who long pondered the fate of his old Army buddy found him in the oddest of places: living practically next door for the last 18 years. Dave Brown heard that his basic training buddy Roger Watson had been wounded in Vietnam, which was true—Watson lost a kidney and suffered other organ damage after being shot early in his tour in 1968, the Detroit Free Press reports. Brown even checked the Vietnam Veterans Memorial to see whether his fellow combat engineer demolition specialist, who trained with him at Ft. Hood in Texas, had died. Then Brown's wife, Peggy, befriended a kitty-corner neighbor over the back fence, chatting with him about their mutual interest in birds. She was also intrigued by the Purple Heart license plate on his car, and 9th Infantry Vietnam Veterans cap in his car's rear window. So she asked about them in an email. Soon, the exclamation points were flying: "Small world!!!!!!" and "Wow!!!!" are among the words in their email exchange as they figured out who was who. A backyard meeting was quickly arranged, and Brown "came running out of the house, over to the corner, had to beat back the brush, we just shook hands and couldn’t believe it," Watson tells WXYZ. "It was just an incredible feeling, just amazing." Reclaiming their friendship after nearly half a century, they plan to "share a beer every now and then" and see "a lot more of each other," says Watson. "Grow very old and watch our grandchildren grow up," adds Dave. "It's just great to see him alive. It makes me happy." (Read about a guy apparently outed as "fake" Army veteran.)

final_summary:
In the wake of the devastating Orlando massacre on Sunday, Senator Marco Rubio (R., Fla.) found himself engaged in a candid conversation with long-time friend and fellow Floridian, Lieutenant Governor Carlos Lopez-Cantera, about their respective futures in politics. The two men, who have been friends for 20 years, met at the scene of the tragedy, where they witnessed the aftermath of the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history. According to Lopez-Cantera, he urged Rubio to reconsider running for his Senate seat, citing the gravity of the situation and the significance of the upcoming election. This conversation marked a turning point for Rubio, who had previously shown reluctance to run for re-election despite pressure from Senate leaders and donors.

Lopez-Cantera's words struck a chord with Rubio, who had been weighing his options and considering a potential exit from the Senate. The lieutenant governor emphasized that his decision to bring up the topic was not driven by personal ambition, but rather a sense of duty to the country and the importance of the election. "This is bigger than me," Lopez-Cantera said. "And this isn't about me. It's about our country and this election." With the filing deadline for the Senate race looming on June 24, Rubio's decision will have significant implications for the GOP's chances of retaining control of the Senate.

The conversation between Rubio and Lopez-Cantera has sparked intense speculation about the senator's future, with many believing that he will ultimately choose not to run for re-election. Longtime friends of Rubio suggest that the allure of a higher-paying job and the opportunity to spend more time with his family will likely sway his decision. However, others argue that Rubio's commitment to public service and his desire to continue making a difference in the Senate will keep him in the running.

As the debate continues, Lopez-Cantera remains resolute in his own bid for the Senate seat, emphasizing that his decision to discuss the matter with Rubio was not a ploy to secure a deal or gain an advantage. "There is no deal. There is no plan," he insisted. Despite the rumors and speculation surrounding the two friends, Lopez-Cantera maintains that his goal is to serve the people of Florida and the nation, not to advance his own career.

Miami-Dade County Commissioner Steve Bovo, whose wife works as a policy adviser for Rubio, said he wasn't surprised by Lopez-Cantera's discussion. Bovo served in the Florida House when Lopez-Cantera was majority leader just after Rubio left as Florida House speaker, a post he held in 2007-08. "Carlos is not only thinking about their friendship but the country," Bovo said. "We all know each other, go to each other's kids' birthday parties. In the difficult business we're in, we separate the friendship and the politics. But we never do anything to endanger our friendship."

On Wednesday, Rubio told reporters in the Capitol that he would take time to consider his decision, stating that he would head home later that week to spend time with his family. "If there's been a change in our status, I'll be sure to let everyone know," he said. Meanwhile, the Republican primary for the Senate seat remains crowded, with several candidates vying for the nomination. U.S. Representative David Jolly is set to announce his plans on Friday, while defense contractor Todd Wilcox has indicated that he will stay in the race regardless of Rubio's decision. The outcome of the election will have far-reaching consequences for the balance of power in the Senate, and Rubio's choice will undoubtedly play a crucial role in determining the fate of the GOP's efforts to retain control of the chamber.

The prospect of Rubio doing so gained traction in the wake of the Orlando shooting, but he continued to insist that he wouldn't run against his friend already in the race, Carlos Lopez-Cantera. The incumbent told reporters on Wednesday that he'd go home this week to spend time with his family and consider his next steps. Rumors are swirling that the pair have some sort of plan—perhaps for Rubio to win the seat over a Democrat (Republicans believe he has the best shot), then resign and allow Lopez-Cantera to run before the 2020 presidential campaign. But Lopez-Cantera says that's not the case. "Until I mentioned this, he was really committed to spending time with his kids, being able to be home for more than 10 days without having to go somewhere," he says. As of now, "nothing has changed,” he adds. "I'm still running. Marco isn't." Rubio's decision isn’t expected until the weekend at the earliest, per the Wall Street Journal; he has until June 24 to file for re-election. If he does so, Lopez-Cantera won't run, says Politico.

final_summary:
Late Wednesday, Twitter announced plans to overhaul its process for verifying accounts, resulting in the removal of the coveted blue checkmark from several high-profile far-right users. Among those affected was Tim "Baked Alaska" Gionet, a former BuzzFeed staffer turned alt-right figure, who was permanently banned from the platform. In response to the loss of his verification, Gionet resorted to livestreaming his meltdown at an In-N-Out Burger, where he randomly screamed at innocent bystanders.

Laura Loomer, a self-proclaimed independent journalist and former employee of Project Veritas, a right-wing sting operation led by James O'Keefe, took the loss of her verification particularly hard. Loomer, who gained notoriety for disrupting a New York City performance of "Julius Caesar" to provoke a reaction, suggested that the situation was eerily reminiscent of the beginning of a film about the Holocaust. When a verified Twitter user pointed out that the comparison was unfounded, Loomer doubled down on her stance.

Interestingly, other notable far-right figures, including neo-Nazis Richard Spencer and Jason Kessler, who organized the deadly white supremacist rally in Charlottesville, retained their verification status despite being stripped of it. This development has led some to question whether Twitter's efforts to address harassment and abuse on the platform are merely superficial, aimed at avoiding the appearance of endorsing extremist ideologies without actually preventing them from using the service.

In a series of tweets, Twitter explained that "verification was meant to authenticate identity & voice ... [but] verification has long been perceived as an endorsement. We gave verified accounts visual prominence on the service which deepened this perception. We should have addressed this earlier but did not prioritize the work as we should have." General verification requests had been put on ice as of Nov. 9 pending a review of the program, and the Guardian reports that Twitter's new policy specifies that accounts that promote hate, support hate groups, or harass users will see verification revoked. Loomer's response to her notice of verification removal including tweeting "Translation: I'm a conservative" and, per Splinter, comparing her situation to that of Jews during the Holocaust.

final_summary:
Celebrated author Alice Walker, known for her Pulitzer Prize-winning novel The Color Purple, has once again found herself embroiled in controversy for her association with David Icke, a British author and public speaker known for his conspiracy theories and anti-Semitic views. In an interview with The New York Times Book Review, Walker listed Icke's book "And the Truth Shall Set You Free" as one of the four books on her nightstand, sparking widespread criticism and accusations of anti-Semitism.

Icke's book is described as an "unhinged anti-Semitic conspiracy tract" that promotes a range of anti-Semitic tropes, including the idea that a shadowy cabal controls the world and that this cabal is Jewish. The book draws heavily on the infamous anti-Semitic pamphlet, The Protocols of the Elders of Zion, and features quotes and assertions that are deeply hostile to Jews and the Talmud. Icke's views on Jews are not limited to this book, however - he has a long history of making anti-Semitic statements, including calling the Talmud "among the most appallingly racist documents on the planet."

Walker's association with Icke is not new, and she has previously praised his work and ideas in interviews and online posts. In 2013, she wrote a glowing review of Icke's book "Human Race Get Off Your Knees," describing it as an "amazing book" that allowed her to understand the "root of the incredible evil that has engulfed our planet." She has also shared Icke's content on social media, including a video interview with Alex Jones, and promoted one of his lectures to her readers on YouTube. 

Despite her long history of promoting Icke's work, Walker has never been held accountable by elite cultural critics, and her association with Icke has been largely ignored by publications like The New York Times. However, the paper's failure to challenge Walker's recommendation of Icke's book has sparked outrage, with many accusing the newspaper of perpetuating anti-Semitism in Britain.

Yair Rosenberg, a senior writer at Tablet, criticized The New York Times for failing to provide context or qualification for Walker's recommendation, noting that the paper's policy is to allow subjects to speak for themselves without editorial intervention. However, Rosenberg argued that this approach is inadequate when dealing with anti-Semitic content, and that the paper has a responsibility to challenge and contextualize such views.

Walker's defenders have sought to deflect criticism by pointing to her support for the Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions (BDS) movement against Israel, arguing that her anti-Israel views are being unfairly conflated with anti-Semitism. However, this argument is not convincing, as many anti-Zionists are also anti-Semites, and Walker's views on Jews and the Talmud are clearly anti-Semitic.

The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) has long monitored Icke's activities and has condemned his anti-Semitic rhetoric. The Anti-Defamation League has stated that Icke's views are not just anti-Semitic but also contribute to a broader culture of hate and intolerance.

The controversy surrounding Walker and Icke highlights the need for greater scrutiny and accountability from cultural institutions like The New York Times. By failing to challenge and contextualize anti-Semitic views, these institutions risk perpetuating hatred and intolerance. As Rosenberg noted, "Not publishing anti-Semitic book recommendations unchallenged in the New York Times would be a good place to start."
anchors: The New York Times Book Review

reference: – The New York Times is standing by its Q&A with Pulitzer-winning author Alice Walker despite complaints about her comments on a book by an accused anti-Semite. At Tablet, Yair Rosenberg writes that the book referencing The Protocols of the Elders of Zion, a text accusing Jewish people of a plot for global domination, "is an unhinged anti-Semitic conspiracy tract written by one of Britain's most notorious anti-Semites." But Amy Russo at HuffPo notes the paper failed to provide context in an editor's note, "thereby allowing [Icke's] name to be promoted unchecked." Rosenberg argues the column only ensures "racism is disseminated to more people." Walker also said this of the book: "In Icke’s books there is the whole of existence, on this planet and several others, to think about." Her comments are "especially corrosive because the anti-Jewish conspiracies she uplifted and adopted are part of the same white supremacist power structure she so deftly fought through her written work in the past," adds Jewish filmmaker Rebecca Pierce. (Walker once blocked an Israeli publisher from printing her book.)

final_summary:
In the Alipore zoo in Kolkata, formerly known as Calcutta, a BBC report highlights a peculiar situation involving a 10-year-old white tiger named Vishal. According to officials, Vishal has shown little interest in mating with his potential partner, an eight-year-old tigress named Rupa, despite repeated attempts to encourage him since the past six months. The efforts to mate the pair began last year, with zoo officials administering various treatments, including vitamins A and E, and libido enhancers, in an attempt to boost Vishal's libido. However, none of these measures have yielded results.

Dayanarayan Banerjee, a senior veterinary officer at the zoo, attributes Vishal's behavior to his shyness, likely caused by growing up in captivity without interacting with the opposite sex. This theory is supported by experts from the forest and wildlife department, who believe that Vishal's demeanor is not indicative of a lack of virility, but rather a result of his limited socialization.

The breeding program at Alipore zoo is crucial for maintaining a healthy population of big cats, as many of the current residents are aging and nearing the end of their reproductive prime. The last successful breeding of a tiger cub occurred, and officials are eager to revive the program. Despite the challenges posed by Vishal's reluctance, zoo authorities remain optimistic and are exploring alternative options, including introducing him to a younger tigress named Rani, who is described as "young and full of vigor."

Banerjee expressed concerns that Vishal may be too old to mate effectively, citing that the ideal age range for tiger mating is typically around five to six years old, with seven to eight-year-olds also being viable candidates. Beyond the age of 10 years, mating becomes increasingly difficult. As the zoo continues to navigate this complex issue, officials are hopeful that a new pairing may finally yield the desired outcome.

reference: – Male tigers are a lot like human men when it comes to sex. They're shy and awkward about their bodies if they haven't spent a lot of time around female tigers. They take libido enhancers when they get older. And, if a partner their own age isn't doing it for them, they consider trading them in for a younger, prettier tiger. BBC News reports India's Alipore zoo is having a difficult time getting its 10-year-old white tiger Vishal to mate. The zoo has tried vitamins and even de-worming him; nothing seems to be working. Experts are blaming the fact that Vishal was born in captivity, meaning he doesn't have much experience with females and might not understand the biological signals his body is sending, according to the Times of India. Plus, Vishal is at the outer limits of breeding age for tigers. This is apparently frustrating to Rupa, a female white tiger the zoo has assigned to breed with Vishal. While in heat, Rupa will chase, touch, and scratch Vishal, but he "runs away with his tail between his legs," a veterinary consultant tells the Times. Zoo staff have been trying to mate the two tigers for six months, and it's important they succeed because the zoo's tiger population is getting older and hasn't had a birth in more than a decade. Now, staff are considering trying Vishal out with a much younger Bengal tiger named Rani, who is described as having "a very beautiful face," the BBC reports. Ouch, Rupa.

final_summary:
As of today, individuals who consume at least half a bottle of wine or three pints a night will have access to a new pill aimed at reducing their alcohol consumption on a daily basis. This decision comes after the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) published its formal guidance on nalmefene, a medication that decreases the urge to drink and can be prescribed alongside counseling support. According to the World Health Organisation's guidelines, regular drinking of 7.5 units a day for men and five units a day for women is considered excessive, making them eligible for this treatment.

Nalmefene, also known as Selincro, works by helping patients gradually decrease their alcohol intake and is not suitable for those requiring immediate abstinence due to severe alcohol problems. The pill can be taken up to once a day and costs £3. It is designed to be used when needed to prevent the desire to drink.

Lyndsey Dudley, a spokesperson for Nice, likened the medication to a nicotine patch, providing support for individuals looking to cut down on their alcohol consumption, acknowledging that some days may be easier than others. The organisation estimates that over two million Britons are mildly dependent on alcohol, with approximately 35,000 people expected to be prescribed nalmefene within the first year. However, data from Scotland, where nalmefene was approved for use in October last year, shows that only 53 people received the medication, indicating that the actual number may be lower.

According to information provided by Lundbeck, the manufacturer of nalmefene, potential candidates for the medication include individuals such as Sue, who enjoys a glass of wine after work but often consumes an entire bottle while cooking and eating with her family several times a week. Professor Carole Longson, director of Nice's Health Technology Evaluation Centre, stated that many people struggle with their relationship with alcohol despite maintaining stable lifestyles and social lives, highlighting the importance of addressing this issue before it leads to serious health complications.

Professor Longson emphasised that those eligible for nalmefene have already demonstrated a willingness to address their drinking habits by consulting their doctors, engaging with support services, and participating in therapy programs. When combined with psychosocial support, nalmefene is deemed clinically and cost-effective for the NHS compared to psychosocial support alone. In contrast, Professor Jonathan Chick, a medical advisor for Drinkaware, noted that consuming two glasses of wine per day does not constitute mild alcoholism, and nalmefene is intended for individuals who have attempted to cut down on their drinking but continue to exceed low-risk levels.

Data was released yesterday showing that in Scotland, where nalmefene was approved for use in October last year, only 53 people were prescribed the medication. Despite this, Nice predicts that around 35,000 people will be given nalmefene in the first year, based on the assumption that 58,000 individuals will receive psychosocial intervention for mild alcohol dependency, with 60% opting for the medication.
anchors: The National Institute For Health And Care Excellence

In contrast, Professor Jonathan Chick, a medical advisor for Drinkaware, noted that consuming two glasses of wine per day does not constitute mild alcoholism, and nalmefene is intended for individuals who have attempted to cut down on their drinking but continue to exceed low-risk levels.

Data was released yesterday showing that in Scotland, where nalmefene was approved for use in October last year, only 53 people were prescribed the medication. Despite this, Nice predicts that around 35,000 people will be given nalmefene in the first year, based on the assumption that 58,000 individuals will receive psychosocial intervention for mild alcohol dependency, with 60% opting for the medication.

reference: – Those Brits who want to cut down on their alcohol intake—and can spare about $5 a day—now have access to a pill to reduce their craving. UK officials have approved the drug nalmefene for use along with counseling to help reduce alcohol dependency, the International Business Times reports. The official approval means people who fit the right profile can request the prescription drug. "Those who could be prescribed nalmefene have already taken the first big steps by visiting their doctor, engaging with support services, and taking part in therapy programs," says a researcher. Nalmefene, which can be taken once a day, helps people feel less of an urge to drink, experts say. It's considered appropriate for many people drinking the equivalent of at least half a bottle of wine daily, the Guardian reports. Plenty of potential candidates to take the drug "probably don’t even recognize themselves as an alcoholic," says a health official, who compares it to a nicotine patch. But since nalmefene is intended to be used to gradually reduce alcohol intake, it's not a good solution for people who need to quit right away, the Guardian notes.

final_summary:
WASHINGTON - On Thursday, Senator Ted Cruz (R-Texas) delivered a scathing critique of President Obama's nominee for Attorney General, Loretta Lynch, on the Senate floor, expressing concerns over her stance on immigration and executive overreach. Cruz argued that Lynch's responses during her confirmation hearing demonstrated a willingness to disregard the law, echoing the approach taken by outgoing Attorney General Eric Holder. In a 10-minute speech, Cruz emphasized that Lynch's commitment to supporting Obama's executive actions on immigration made her unsuitable for the position. Despite his strong opposition, Cruz ultimately missed the final vote on Lynch's confirmation, citing a prior commitment in Texas, although his office initially stated that he had voted against cloture, the decisive procedural vote that paved the way for her confirmation.

Cruz's decision to skip the vote drew attention, particularly given his previous criticism of Holder and his calls for the Attorney General's resignation. However, Cruz's own attendance record has come under scrutiny, with reports indicating that he has missed a significant number of hearings and votes, including a recent vote on a sex trafficking bill that passed unanimously without his presence.

Lynch's confirmation ultimately proceeded with a 56-43 vote, with Senator John Cornyn voting against her nomination. Cruz's absence from the vote was attributed to a scheduled trip to Dallas for a fundraiser, according to his spokesperson, Catherine Frazier. Frazier maintained that Cruz's opposition to Lynch's nomination remained unwavering, emphasizing that his vote against cloture was the most crucial step in the process. Cruz's stance on Lynch's confirmation reflects his broader concerns about the role of the Attorney General and the potential for partisan lawlessness, a theme he has consistently raised throughout his career.

Cruz's comments on the Senate floor highlighted the perceived similarities between Lynch's views and those of Holder, whom he has repeatedly criticized for disregarding constitutional authority. By opposing Lynch's nomination, Cruz aimed to underscore the distinction between the Democratic and Republican majorities, arguing that the outcome of her confirmation would demonstrate whether the Republican-led Senate would uphold the rule of law or acquiesce to the administration's agenda. As a candidate for the 2016 presidential nomination, Cruz's stance on Lynch's confirmation further underscores his commitment to challenging the Obama administration's policies.

reference: – Today's vote in the Senate to make Loretta Lynch the next attorney general was 56-43. So who was the lone missing senator out of 100? Ted Cruz, which is generating some head scratches considering that he spoke for 10 minutes on the floor of the Senate prior to the vote pleading with his colleagues to reject her. It turns out that Cruz had to catch a flight back to Texas for a fundraiser before the final vote, reports the Dallas Morning News. His spokesperson is downplaying the move, noting that Cruz voted against the procedure for ending a filibuster that cleared the way for a final vote. "He voted for cloture, that was the vote that mattered," says Rick Tyler. "He made the case against her, he voted against her in cloture, and he didn’t prevail." Still, the unusual combination of the presidential candidate blasting Lynch and then being the only senator to skip the vote is making headlines at Politico, BuzzFeed, the Washington Post, and elsewhere. As for Cruz's objection to Lynch, he says she "embraced the lawlessness" that marked the tenure of Eric Holder.

final_summary:
Over the weekend, a harrowing scene unfolded on the Bronx River Parkway in Mount Vernon, Westchester County, as a 6-year-old boy with autism managed to drive a miniature battery-powered ATV onto the highway on Sunday evening. According to eyewitness accounts, the boy, who was initially with his family near a park on South 7th Avenue, slipped away and got onto the toy vehicle before taking off towards the parkway. Witnesses described the boy's mother frantically searching for him, asking fellow park-goers if anyone had seen her son.

As the boy continued driving on the parkway, several drivers slowed down to block him from other traffic, creating a makeshift barrier to prevent potential collisions. Among those who intervened were Ana Rubio and her husband, Joel, who were hailed as heroes for their quick thinking and bravery. Rubio recalled holding the shaken boy in her arms, with the child repeatedly crying out for his mother. 

"It seemed like the child wanted to continue driving, he was trying to merge on the middle lane of the highway," Rubio stated, describing the boy's apparent confusion about his surroundings. Another Good Samaritan, Joel Reyes, corroborated Rubio's account, stating that the initial person to encounter the boy was visibly shaken, having narrowly avoided hitting the child.

Genoveva Burgos, a witness at the park, recounted how the boy's mother had been desperately searching for her son, only to receive news that he had been spotted on the parkway. By the time police received multiple 911 calls about the situation, the boy had already reached the northbound ramp of the parkway near Oak Street. It was here that Rubio and other motorists successfully surrounded the boy, preventing further harm until police arrived.

Westchester County police spokesperson Kieran O'Leary confirmed that the boy was eventually taken into custody and transported to Montefiore Mount Vernon Hospital for observation, where he was found to be uninjured. The incident remains under investigation, with authorities praising the swift actions of the Good Samaritans who helped prevent a potentially disastrous outcome.

reference: – Drivers on the Bronx River Parkway were a little surprised to see a 6-year-old driving his miniature ATV beside them last night. The boy's mom was even more surprised to discover him missing. It started on a family trip to a park in Mount Vernon, New York, where the autistic boy rode off unnoticed at around 7pm, the Journal News reports. "They realized pretty quickly he was gone but couldn't lay eyes on him," a police spokesman said. Bystanders say the mother was frantically calling for her boy as kids ran back and forth looking for him, 7Online reports. Soon he had zoomed onto the northbound parkway and was even trying to merge into the middle lane, police and witnesses say. So drivers boxed him in and grabbed him just as police arrived. "He was like, 'I need to go home, mom, mom,' all he kept saying was 'mom,'" said Ana Rubio, one of the drivers. Another was said to be shaking with goosebumps, telling people, "I almost hit a kid." As for the boy, he seemingly "didn't know he was on a highway, he just wanted to go home," said Rubio. He was taken to a hospital first and "found to be OK," reports 7Online, but police are still investigating. (Read about an autistic boy allegedly kept in a cage.)

final_summary:
Sally Hootnick, a resident of Manlius, New York, experienced a frustrating ordeal after she accidentally left her iPad on a plane seat during an American Airlines flight that landed in New York City, N.Y. According to The Post-Standard, Hootnick initially tried to recover her device by calling the airline's lost and found department and leaving messages, but to no avail. Undeterred, she utilized the tracking feature of her MobileMe account, which allowed her to locate her iPad through GPS. To her surprise, the device was not at the airport but rather at an address in Long Island, N.Y.

Hootnick contacted both American Airlines and the Port Authority of New York & New Jersey, informing them of her iPad's location and expressing her desire to retrieve it. However, despite her efforts, the authorities failed to take action for these days. Feeling frustrated, Hootnick decided to take matters into her own hands by sending a message to her iPad, stating that she knew its location and had already informed the police. This move seemed to prompt the thief to start moving the device around, with Hootnick observing it travel from New York City to L.A., back to New York City, to San Francisco, and finally back to New York City.

Convinced that the thief was likely an airline employee due to the frequent movement of the device, Hootnick employed another application to make her iPad emit a loud "beep" every two minutes and send messages to the thief, reiterating that she knew their identity and had contacted the authorities. Over the course of a month, Hootnick exchanged emails with American Airlines, eventually prompting a representative from the airline's corporate security team to reach out to her. Additionally, she had two conversations with a police detective who was assigned to investigate the case by American Airlines.

The next day, Hootnick emailed the airline that she was going to contact a consumer protection agency. The next day, a person from American Airlines corporate security called, asking for information. Although the case remains under investigation, Hootnick has since replaced her iPad and disabled the wireless service on the original device, rendering it untrackable. Despite this, she believes that the thief is unlikely to be able to sell or use the device, as its serial number will flag it as stolen if anyone attempts to activate new wireless services.
anchors: The Port Authority Of New York & New Jersey

reference: – There's an app for pretty much everything—except cutting through bureaucracy. A woman who mistakenly left her iPad on a plane was able to track the whereabouts of the device, but couldn't convince authorities to follow up on it, reports the Syracuse Post-Standard, via the Consumerist. The woman was fairly certain she left the iPad on a plane after deboarding in New York City, and after leaving messages with American Airlines, she used the GPS function of MobileMe to find that the iPad was in a house on Long Island. She contacted airline and law-enforcement authorities with the exact location, but no action was taken. She suspects an airline employee pocketed the iPad because it then made several trips back and forth between JFK airport and California. She can't trace it anymore because she replaced the iPad and shut off wireless service to the old one. At one point, she used an app to make it beep every two minutes. “I just wanted to harass who ever took it,” she said. “I didn’t want him to enjoy it if he was using it.”

final_summary:
USA Today reported that in the aftermath of the foiled Christmas Day bombing attempt on a Northwest Airlines flight, an unclassified report detailing the missed clues that could have potentially prevented the incident is set to be released on Thursday. According to White House national security adviser James Jones, Americans will experience a sense of shock upon learning about the available information that was not acted upon, including patterns of behavior exhibited by the alleged bomber, Umar Farouk Abdulmutallab. The 23-year-old Nigerian man, who has been indicted on charges of attempted murder and using a weapon of mass destruction, had traveled to Yemen where he claimed to have received training and equipment from a group affiliated with Al-Qaeda.

Abdulmutallab's father had previously warned American officials at the U.S. Embassy in Nigeria about his son's potential involvement with extremist ideologies, yet his visa was not revoked, and he was not added to the "no-fly" list. This oversight has raised concerns among lawmakers, with Senate Intelligence Chairman Dianne Feinstein advocating for a more comprehensive no-fly list that includes individuals with even a reasonable suspicion of ties to terrorism.

The White House plans to unveil new measures to prevent similar incidents in the future, with Jones emphasizing that the solutions lie in "tweaking" existing systems rather than implementing a complete overhaul. These proposed changes aim to alleviate the workload of intelligence analysts, allowing them to effectively identify and respond to potential threats.

Meanwhile, investigations into Abdulmutallab's connections to Al-Qaeda continue to unfold. A senior Yemeni official revealed that Abdulmutallab met with Anwar Al-Awlaki, a radical American Muslim cleric of Yemeni descent, in London before traveling to Yemen. Al-Awlaki has been linked to several high-profile terror attacks, including the Fort Hood shooting in November, which resulted in the deaths of 13 people. The cleric's involvement in these incidents has led some to label him a key figure in Al-Qaeda's operations.

On Wednesday, in Detroit, Abdulmutallab appeared in court, facing charges related to the attempted bombing. As the investigation into the Christmas Day bombing continues, President Obama is expected to address the nation regarding the incident and outline new steps to prevent future terrorist attacks. The White House's efforts come amidst growing pressure from the international community, particularly from the US, to take decisive action against Al-Qaeda in Yemen.

In an interview with USA Today, Jones doesn’t downplay the miscues, saying President Obama is “legitimately and correctly alarmed” that intelligence wasn’t acted upon. Combined with the Fort Hood shooting, “that’s two strikes,” says Jones. “He certainly doesn’t want that third strike.” Meanwhile, a Yemeni official confirmed a link between those attacks, telling the BBC that alleged would-be bomber Umar Farouk Abdulmutallab had met with radical American cleric Anwar al-Awlaki, who authorities believe was also in contact with the Fort Hood shooter. Abdulmutallab was recruited in London, the official said, then flew to Yemen to meet Awlaki. But he noted that the explosives were from Nigeria, not Yemen.

final_summary:
In a groundbreaking move, National Louis University, a private institution based in Chicago, has partnered with Groupon to offer a unique opportunity for aspiring educators. Starting on Tuesday, the university will make available a heavily discounted graduate-level introduction to teaching course, designed specifically for individuals with little to no experience in the field. This innovative initiative aims to attract potential teachers by offering a significant reduction in tuition fees - nearly 60 percent off the regular price of $2,232, bringing the cost down to $950.

The three-credit course, which will be taught at National Louis University's Chicago campus located at 122 S. Michigan Ave, is open to anyone holding an undergraduate degree. However, it's essential to note that while this course counts towards a graduate degree, students will still need to complete an additional 33 credits at full price to earn their degree. The course will meet for three hours every Monday evening, starting from late September and running until December.

According to Jocelyn Zivin, Vice President of Marketing and Communications for National Louis University, the primary goal of this initiative is to encourage prospective teachers to gain a deeper understanding of the realities of the profession. By taking this course, students can assess whether they have what it takes to commit to a teaching career. As Zivin explained, there are various factors within the K-12 system that discourage individuals from pursuing teaching degrees, and this course offers a chance for them to explore their passion before making a long-term commitment.

Julie Mossler, Groupon's Communications Director, views this partnership as an exciting opportunity for their subscribers to take the first step towards a potentially new career path. National Louis University becomes the first academic institution to utilize Groupon as a means to boost student interest in its programs. The deal will be available until either Wednesday or when all 25 spots are filled, whichever comes first, with the sale tipping at 15 buyers.

final_summary:
On Sunday morning, two explosions rocked two churches in Las Cruces, New Mexico, sending shockwaves through the community without causing any injuries. The first explosion occurred at approximately 8:20 a.m. at Calvary Baptist Church, where a device planted in a mailbox detonated just as congregants began to gather for morning services. The blast was loud enough to shake the building, and about 30 people were inside at the time.

According to eyewitnesses, including church member Dennis Llewellyn, a former Marine special forces member, the explosion was a real bomb, with recognizable parts visible among the debris. Llewellyn described the device as having an Eveready battery, further indicating its destructive intent.

Twenty minutes later, at 8:40 a.m., another explosion occurred at Holy Cross Catholic Church, located just three miles away. This time, the device was placed in a trash can outside the church's entrance, shattering the glass front and startling worshipers in the midst of their morning Mass. The congregation, numbering around 200 people, was fortunate to avoid injury due to the timing of the explosion, which coincided with the communion portion of the service. Normally, someone is stationed at the entrance, but everyone had entered the building for the service, saving lives.

Monsignor John Anderson, the pastor of Holy Cross, recounted the harrowing experience, stating that he was in the middle of saying the words "take and eat, this is my body" when the explosion occurred. Despite the chaos, he continued to lead the service, finishing the Lord's Prayer before sending a deacon to investigate the noise.

The Las Cruces Police Department, led by Spokesman Dan Trujillo, is investigating the incidents, assisted by various local and federal agencies, including the New Mexico State Police, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and the Department of Homeland Security. Trujillo emphasized that the devices were "dangerous, much more dangerous than just fireworks or firecrackers" and intended to cause harm.

Robin Zielinski, a photographer for The Sun-News, captured images of the aftermath of the explosions, providing a visual representation of the devastation. 

New Mexico Governor Susana Martinez condemned the attacks, calling the perpetrator a "coward" who had failed to instill fear in the community. State Police Chief Pete Kassetas echoed this sentiment, stating that the explosions certainly provoked terror, although he stopped short of labeling them as an act of terrorism.

In the aftermath of the incidents, Holy Cross Catholic Church has announced plans to increase security measures, including adding patrols to the parking lot and installing cameras at the front entrance. Meanwhile, the community rallied together, with faith leaders calling for forgiveness and unity during a Sunday evening event at Pioneer Park.

A sign held by Aaliyah Doninguez, a parishioner, informed her community about the cancellation of Sunday's Mass at Holy Cross Catholic Church, reflecting the resilience and solidarity of the Las Cruces community in the face of adversity.

reference: – Police are investigating a pair of blasts that rocked two New Mexico churches where parishioners had gathered yesterday morning, though there were no injuries and damage was "relatively minor," authorities tell NBC News. One explosion came from a mailbox outside an administrative entrance to Calvary Baptist Church in Las Cruces at about 8:20am local time, reports the Washington Post. About 30 people had gathered inside and witnesses say they felt the building shake. Twenty minutes later, the pastor at Holy Cross Catholic Church, three miles away, said he heard a "pow" while passing out communion. "I didn't know if it was a shotgun blast…but it was very loud," he tells the Las Cruces News. The bomb exploded in a trash can outside the church's glass front doors, which shattered. "I'm just thankful to God nobody was standing by the door, because there's usually always somebody standing there," the pastor says. "Ten minutes later we would have been leaving and standing around that space." A churchgoer at Calvary Baptist—which is on the same street as several other churches that weren't targeted—says he recognized bomb parts at the scene, including an Eveready battery. A police rep says only that the two devices "were not fireworks" and "were intended, I believe, to do harm and could have done harm to people." Police add the blasts are considered related and authorities have warned other nearby churches "to be on the lookout for anything suspicious." Both churches remained closed yesterday afternoon, while classes at Holy Cross' adjoining school were canceled today.

final_summary:
ABC News reported that a massive global email virus, dubbed "Here You Have," swept across the globe on Thursday, crippling email services at numerous high-profile organizations, including NASA, Comcast, Disney, and the Florida Department of Transportation. The virus, which was first spotted in August, was characterized by an email with the subject line "Here You Have" or "Just For You," containing a link that appeared to be a PDF document. However, upon closer inspection, the link was actually a malicious program designed to install malware on unsuspecting victims' computers.

According to McAfee, a leading cybersecurity firm, the virus was not a traditional worm, meaning it required human interaction to spread, but rather a social engineering attack that relied on users clicking on the malicious link. Once activated, the malware could spread to connected computers and USB drives, causing significant disruptions to email services.

SecureWorks, an Atlanta-based security firm, linked the virus to a cyber-jihad organization called Brigades of Tariq ibn Ziyad, citing a 2008 posting from a known Libyan hacker who aimed to unite like-minded hackers in a cyber-jihad. The hacker's goal, as stated in the posting, was to "penetrate U.S. agencies."

ABC News' Jason Ryan, Marisa Bramwell, Lee Ferran, and Sidney Wright contributed to the initial reporting on the story. McAfee Vice President of Threat Research, Dmitri Alperovitch, confirmed that the company was investigating the attack, describing it as an email-based worm that propagated via a link claiming to be a PDF document. The company later published a report identifying the virus as a Trojan and stating that the risk level for both home and corporate email users was low.

Symantec, another prominent cybersecurity firm, noted similarities between the "Here You Have" virus and the infamous "Anna Kournikova" virus from 2001, which also used the same subject line. The company speculated that the threat may have originated from a botnet and was targeting numerous companies indiscriminately.

As the virus spread, ABC News reported that the US-CERT and DHS National Cyber Security Division were working together to analyze the malware and develop mitigation strategies. Several federal departments and agencies, including NASA, experienced the virus, while the Florida Department of Transportation reported that six other agencies in the state were also affected.

Adobe Systems warned of vulnerabilities in its Adobe Reader software, suggesting that hackers may have exploited these weaknesses to send the malicious emails in PDF format. The virus caused significant disruptions, with some organizations forced to shut down their email services temporarily, but fortunately, no major operational impacts were reported.

Employees took to social media to express frustration and humorously comment on the situation, with NASA's Lunar Science Institute tweeting, "Houston, we have a problem... it's called spam."
