reference: – An email spam virus swept through corporate America's inboxes yesterday, causing servers to be shut down at major operations including Comcast, AIG, Disney, and NASA. The virus spread through emails with the subject line "Here you have," which contained a link that downloads a virus that sends the same email to everybody in a user's address book. The Department of Homeland Security is investigating, reports ABC News. Security experts say anybody receiving the "here you have" email should delete it and contact their IT departments. They stress that people should never click links in emails from unknown people, or even in suspect emails from people they know. "The fact that it managed to spread widely through various multinational businesses doesn't say a lot for the security savvy of the workers," notes Neil Rubenking at PC World.

final_summary:
A new study published in the journal Climatic Change warns that rising global temperatures due to climate change will significantly impact the aviation industry, making it harder for many aircraft to take off in the coming decades. According to the study, conducted by researchers Ethan Coffel and Radley Horton from Columbia University, during the hottest parts of the day, 10 to 30 percent of fully loaded planes may have to remove some fuel, cargo, or passengers, or wait for cooler hours to fly. This is because warmer air expands and becomes less dense, reducing the lift generated by an airplane's wings, making it difficult for them to take off safely.

The study, which analyzed data from 19 major airports worldwide, including those in the US, Europe, the UAE, and Asia, found that some airports will be more severely impacted than others. New York's LaGuardia Airport, with its short runways, and Dubai International Airport, due to its extremely high temperatures, are among the airports that will face significant challenges. On the other hand, airports like New York's John F. Kennedy International Airport, London's Heathrow, and Paris' Charles de Gaulle are expected to be less affected.

The study estimates that if greenhouse gas emissions continue unabated, fuel capacities and payload weights will have to be reduced by as much as 4 percent on the hottest days for some aircraft. This means that an average 160-seat aircraft may have to remove 12 or 13 passengers to ensure safe takeoff. The researchers also note that some aircraft models, such as the Boeing 737-800, will be more severely impacted than others.

In recent heatwaves, the effects of extreme heat on air travel have already been seen in practice. Last month, American Airlines canceled over 40 flights out of Phoenix due to extreme heat, while major airlines delayed or canceled dozens of flights out of Las Vegas and Phoenix airports. The study's findings highlight the urgent need for the aviation industry to adapt to the changing climate.

According to the researchers, some possible solutions to mitigate the impact of heatwaves on air travel include modifying aircraft design, expanding runways, or adjusting flight schedules to depart earlier in the day when it's cooler. However, these measures will require significant investment and planning. As the world's climate continues to warm, the aviation industry will need to find ways to adapt to the changing conditions to avoid costly delays and cancellations.

The study's lead author, Ethan Coffel, emphasized that incorporating climate projections into mid- and long-term plans will be crucial for effective adaptation efforts. Radley Horton, a co-author of the study, noted that the aviation industry needs to start dealing with climate change more aggressively, considering options such as developing engines that produce fewer greenhouse gases and adapting planes to the future climate.

The study's findings have significant implications for the aviation industry, which operates on thin profit margins. The researchers warn that the costs of delays and cancellations could disrupt other sectors of the economy as they trickle down. With global temperatures expected to rise by up to 5.5 degrees Fahrenheit by 2100, the aviation industry will need to take proactive steps to mitigate the impact of heatwaves on air travel.

reference: – Remember in June when it got too hot for planes to take off in Phoenix? A new study suggests stories like that will become quite common in the not-too-distant future. Via Reuters, the study published in Climatic Change estimates that rising temperatures could leave up to one-third of planes grounded during heat waves in the coming decades. The problem centers around the wings’ capacity for lift, which is lessened in warmer conditions. By 2100, worldwide average temperatures are expected to rise by 5.4 degrees Fahrenheit. To safely take off, airlines would need to make adjustments during the hottest part of the day, with 10% to 30% of planes affected. Full flights will either stay grounded until conditions cool down or lighten their loads by removing fuel, cargo, or passengers, per a release at EurekAlert. Think 12 to 13 people asked to leave a 160-person passenger plane. "Our results suggest that weight restriction may impose a non-trivial cost," one of the study’s lead authors says. Wired reports that among the airports studied, New York’s LaGuardia and Washington’s Reagan-National fared poorly because of short runways, as did Dubai International Airport, where extreme heat could force reduced loads up to 55% of the time during peak temperatures. Better news: New York's JFK wouldn't be much affected. Delayed departures won't be the only hassle. Scientists say severe turbulence would become more common. “I’ve yet to see a benefit of climate change to aviation,” says atmospheric scientist Paul Williams, who believes it’s high time for the airline industry to prepare and adapt. (An essay at New York provided a decidedly bleak outlook on climate change.)

final_summary:
On January 9, 2013, it was announced that Lance Armstrong, the former professional cyclist, is set to appear in a 90-minute interview with media mogul Oprah Winfrey on January 17, marking his first televised appearance since being stripped of his seven Tour de France titles in October. The interview, described as "no-holds-barred," will be broadcast on the Oprah Winfrey Network and will cover the doping allegations that led to Armstrong's downfall.

According to the head of the United States Anti-Doping Agency (Usada), Travis Tygart, Armstrong had offered to donate over $150,000 to the organization in 2004, a move deemed a clear conflict of interest. This revelation comes amidst reports that Armstrong is considering publicly admitting to using performance-enhancing drugs and blood transfusions during his cycling career, a move that could potentially lead to his reinstatement under World Anti-Doping Agency rules.

Armstrong's decision to come clean follows years of denials, including his assertion that he passed over 500 drug tests without failing. However, Usada's 202-page report found that Armstrong's former teammates testified to beating tests or avoiding them altogether. The International Cycling Union, which chose not to appeal Usada's lifetime ban, stripped Armstrong of his titles, and the World Anti-Doping Agency agreed with the sanctions, effectively barring him from competition.

The interview with Winfrey has sparked controversy due to her network's ties to Discovery Communications, which sponsored Armstrong's team in 2005. Critics argue that Winfrey may go easy on Armstrong, pointing to her 2008 interview with Marion Jones, another athlete who admitted to using performance-enhancing drugs. However, Kathy LeMond, wife of Tour de France winner Greg LeMond, has offered to educate Winfrey on the culture of professional cycling ahead of the interview.

In preparation for the interview, journalists and cycling enthusiasts have taken to social media to suggest tough questions for Winfrey to ask Armstrong, including queries about his relationship with his children and his involvement in various doping scandals. Armstrong's attorney, Tim Herman, has declined to comment on reports that Armstrong is considering an admission of guilt, which could potentially lead to his reinstatement.

Armstrong's reputation has been marred by allegations of doping, dating back to 2011 when fellow cyclist Floyd Landis made claims against him. Armstrong subsequently sued Usada to stop the investigation but lost the case, leading to his ban from cycling. His subsequent resignation from the Livestrong Foundation and the stripping of his titles marked a significant fall from grace for the once-beloved athlete.

As Armstrong prepares to face the consequences of his actions, the question remains whether he will finally come clean about his involvement in the doping scandal that has haunted him for years. The interview with Winfrey promises to provide insight into the mind of the man who was once a hero to millions but is now facing the possibility of a lifetime ban from competitive sports.

reference: – Lance Armstrong is rumored to be ready to confess to drug use—and it could happen on Oprah's show. The disgraced cyclist will give a "no-holds-barred" interview to Oprah Winfrey that will air on her network next week, CNN reports. In his first formal interview since he was banned from the sport and stripped of his titles for doping, Armstrong will "address the alleged doping scandal, years of accusations of cheating, and charges of lying about the use of performance-enhancing drugs throughout his storied cycling career," the network says in a statement. In a 60 Minutes Sports interview to be aired tonight, the chief of the US Anti-Doping Agency says an Armstrong representative offered to donate around $250,000 to the agency, reports the New York Times. Travis Tygart, who accuses Armstrong of trying to intimidate former teammates, says the 2004 offer represented a clear conflict of interest. "We had no hesitation in rejecting that offer," he says. Tygart says he received death threats during his organization's investigation of Armstrong.

final_summary:
In a dramatic turn of events, the NYPD, along with Mayor Bill de Blasio and Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams, held a press conference on Wednesday to announce the identity of the suspect responsible for the brutal stabbing of two young children in the elevator of a NYCHA building in East New York. The suspect, identified as 27-year-old Daniel St. Hubert, was subsequently arrested in Queens, bringing relief to the community and the families of the victims.

According to authorities, St. Hubert, a 5'9" and 210-pound black male, had a history of violence, having served time for assault and attempted murder. He was released on parole on May 23, but failed to meet with his parole officer earlier that day. The suspect had also been charged with assaulting a police officer and a correction officer, which contributed to his current parole status.

St. Hubert was described as "armed and dangerous" by NYPD Chief of Detectives Robert Boyce, who emphasized that the public should not approach him. The suspect's description was circulated through various media outlets, including a tweet featuring a photo of his distinctive tattoo of a satanic goat's head with horns, etched under the word "Aries."

The investigation began on Sunday, when Prince Joshua "P.J." Avitto and Mikayla Capers were stabbed multiple times in the elevator of the Boulevard Houses on Schenk Avenue in East New York. P.J. tragically succumbed to his injuries, while Mikayla remains in critical condition at New York-Presbyterian Hospital Columbia. Forensic evidence, including DNA from the steak knife used in the attack, linked St. Hubert to the crime scene.

As the investigation unfolded, police released St. Hubert's name and photos, urging the public to come forward with any information. The NYPD also launched a task force to track down the suspect, who had a $69,000 price on his head. Community leaders, including Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams, praised the NYPD for their efforts in apprehending the suspect, saying, "We can breathe a lot better now that the person who we believe committed the heinous crime of stabbing two of our young people is now behind bars." The NYPD's swift action brought closure to the families of the victims, particularly P.J.'s mother, Aricka McClinton, who expressed gratitude to the police and thanked them for catching the perpetrator, saying, "My baby can rest in peace."

The case highlights concerns about safety in public housing developments, with Mayor de Blasio acknowledging the need for increased security measures. Commissioner Bratton announced plans to deploy additional officers to NYCHA buildings, citing the upcoming graduation of new NYPD recruits in July. The incident also raises awareness about the importance of being vigilant and aware of one's surroundings, as emphasized by Rochelle Copeland, who lost her 18-year-old daughter to a similar stabbing in the same area on Friday.

reference: – Police in New York City arrested a 27-year-old man tonight accused of fatally stabbing a 6-year-old boy and injuring his 7-year-old playmate over the weekend, reports Gothamist. Daniel St. Hubert is in custody, the arrest made soon after police publicly identified him as a suspect. As the Daily News explains, the break in the case came when investigators matched DNA found on a knife left at the scene with the state's criminal database. Hubert has a long criminal record for offenses including assault and had been paroled on May 23, reports the New York Times. Police haven't called him a suspect in another fatal stabbing that occurred just two nights earlier, but the investigation is continuing. The same brand of knife—a model discontinued 10 years ago—was left behind in the slaying of 18-year-old Tanaya Copeland, and the man seen fleeing that scene had the same general description as Hubert. The 6-year-old boy who was killed, PJ Avitto, had gotten into an elevator with friend Mikayla Capers in their Brooklyn housing complex before being attacked. Mikayla remains in critical condition.

final_summary:
In the North African country of Tunisia, a wave of protests swept through the streets, culminating in the ousting of President Zine El Abidine Ben Ali, who fled to Saudi Arabia on Friday, marking a significant shift in the Arab world, where authoritarian regimes have long been the norm, following decades of accumulated power in the hands of a highly centralized, American-backed government. This marked a turning point in a region still reeling from the carnage in Iraq, divisions among Palestinians, and Israeli intransigence, as well as the yawning divide between ruler and ruled on almost every question of foreign policy. The protests began in response to rising food prices, unemployment, and a lack of political freedom, issues that resonated deeply with the Tunisian people.

As the situation unfolded, the international community watched closely, with Secretary of State Hillary Rodham Clinton issuing a scathing critique of Arab leadership and the region's stagnant politics. Meanwhile, Al Jazeera broadcast live coverage of the protests, dubbing them "the street creates change." Social media platforms like Facebook played a crucial role in mobilizing the protests, with hashtags like #Tunisia trending worldwide.

Mohamed Bouazizi, a 26-year-old university graduate who immolated himself in a city square in December, became a symbol of the Tunisian revolution. His act of defiance sparked widespread outrage and galvanized the protests, which eventually led to the downfall of Ben Ali's regime. In Egypt, similar sentiments were brewing, with protesters chanting slogans against Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak, echoing the Tunisian cry of "down, down with Hosni Mubarak!"

As the protests gained momentum, the Tunisian government struggled to maintain control. On Saturday, the country's constitutional court ruled that Ben Ali's departure was permanent, paving the way for a new government. Fouad Mebazaa, the president of the lower house of parliament, was sworn in as the new chief of state, promising to hold elections within 60 days. However, the transition was marred by violence, with reports of looting, arson, and clashes between protesters and security forces.

The situation in Tunisia served as a stark reminder of the fragility of authoritarian regimes in the Arab world, where governments have increasingly been criticized for their inability to meet the demands of their populations and their lack of ideology except perpetuating power. As the dust settled, it became clear that the protests had tapped into deeper currents of discontent, fueled by economic woes, corruption, and a sense of disillusionment with the status quo. The fall of Ben Ali sent shockwaves throughout the region, raising hopes that other autocratic leaders might soon follow suit.

In the aftermath of Ben Ali's departure, the Tunisian government faced numerous challenges, including restoring order, addressing the humanitarian crisis, and navigating the complex web of alliances and rivalries within the country. The international community watched closely, with France announcing the launch of investigations into suspicious money transfers between Tunisia and France. As the situation continued to unfold, it remained to be seen whether the new government would be able to consolidate power and usher in a new era of democratic reform.

Meanwhile, the legacy of Ben Ali's rule hung heavy over the country, with many Tunisians expressing fears about the potential for revenge attacks by loyalists. The Interior Ministry, once a bastion of Ben Ali's power, was now a focal point of contention, with reports emerging of security forces loyal to the former regime attempting to discredit the protesters and sow chaos. As the situation continued to evolve, one thing was clear: the people of Tunisia had spoken, and their voices would not be silenced.

reference: – Tunisia got a new interim president today—parliamentary leader Fouad Mebazaa—who is technically the third leader in 24 hours, notes the Los Angeles Times. He takes over from the previous interim president, who briefly held power when longtime leader El Abidine Ben Ali fled the country in the face of mass protests over rising prices, joblessness, corruption, repression, you name it. The storyline continues to shape up as one that could set a historical precedent—an Arab populace rising up against its leader. Anthony Shadid, New York Times: "The most enthusiastic suggested it was the Arab world’s Gdansk, the birthplace of Solidarity in Poland, which heralded the end to Communist rule in Eastern Europe. That seemed premature, particularly because the contours of the government emerging in Tunisia were still unclear." Shadi Hamid, Huffington Post: "No one should underestimate what happened yesterday in Tunisia. If the revolution succeeds, it may very well prove to be one of the most important moments in recent Arab history. It will alter the calculus not only for Arab regimes—who are watching very, very nervously—but for Western powers that have long oriented their Middle East policy around seemingly stable, autocratic governments." Click for the latest as chaos continues on the streets.

final_summary:
In the aftermath of the devastating terrorist attacks in Paris, the international hacking collective Anonymous has escalated its campaign against the Islamic State (IS), declaring "war" on the militant group and vowing to dismantle its online presence. The group, known for its anonymous nature and decentralized structure, has been actively working to identify and expose IS supporters on social media platforms, particularly Twitter, where it has claimed to have taken down over 3,800 pro-IS accounts since the Paris attacks. This latest move marks a significant escalation of Anonymous' efforts, which began in earnest after the Charlie Hebdo attacks in January, when the satirical French magazine was attacked by terrorists. 

Following the Charlie Hebdo attacks, Anonymous launched a campaign dubbed #OpISIS to expose and destroy websites, social media accounts, and email addresses associated with IS. Since then, Anonymous has claimed credit for shutting down several websites and exposing emails, private networks, internet addresses, and over 9,000 Twitter accounts allegedly used by IS activists. As part of its new #OpParis campaign, Anonymous has released a list of over 1,000 Twitter accounts that it claims IS supporters use to spread propaganda. The group has also vowed to launch its "biggest operation ever" against IS, promising to use its cyber skills to "unite humanity" and warning terrorists to "expect massive cyber attacks."

According to Reuters, Anonymous has been busy shutting down social media accounts and websites linked to IS since January, when the group claimed to have taken down nearly 800 Twitter accounts and 50 email addresses associated with IS. However, Anonymous has wrongly identified people, including alleged extremists from IS and Al Qaeda, in the past, highlighting the importance of verifying information before acting on it.

Experts caution that Anonymous' efforts may not be as effective as they seem. Joe Gallop, head of the hacktivism intelligence practice at iSight Partners, notes that IS supporters can easily re-establish new accounts and that the group's use of encrypted communication methods makes it difficult to track their online activities. Moreover, Michael Smith, principal and chief operating officer at Kronos Advisory, warns that uncoordinated efforts by groups like Anonymous can disrupt law enforcement investigations and inadvertently benefit the enemy.

Ghost Security Group, a subgroup of hacktivists that has worked closely with Anonymous, has also been actively tracking IS online activities. The group has handed over valuable information to government officials, which has helped disrupt planned attacks in Tunisia. Smith, who serves as an intermediary between Ghost Security Group and the government, believes that outside support is crucial in combating IS' online presence, but emphasizes the need for coordination with law enforcement agencies to ensure that efforts do not inadvertently aid the enemy.

In a separate development, the French authorities conducted a raid in the Saint-Denis neighborhood of Paris, where three suspected terrorists were believed to be hiding. The operation resulted in gunfire exchanges between the suspects and police, but the outcome remains unclear. Meanwhile, the Islamic State has responded to Anonymous' declaration of war with a statement on its Telegram channel, urging its supporters to take precautions against cyber attacks. The group advised its followers to avoid suspicious links, change their locations using workaround technology, and rename their email addresses to avoid detection.

Al Qaeda, another terrorist organization, has also been a target of Anonymous' efforts, although the extent of their involvement is unclear. The group's decentralized structure and reliance on encrypted communication methods make it challenging to track their online activities. As the conflict between Anonymous and IS continues to unfold, the global community watches with bated breath, wondering whether the hacktivist group's efforts will ultimately make a meaningful difference in the fight against terrorism.

This latest move marks a significant escalation of Anonymous' efforts, which began in earnest after the Charlie Hebdo attacks in January. The group's initial campaign, dubbed #OpISIS, aimed to expose and destroy websites, social media accounts, and email addresses associated with IS. Since then, Anonymous has claimed credit for shutting down several websites and exposing emails, private networks, internet addresses, and over 9,000 Twitter accounts allegedly used by IS activists.

As part of its new #OpParis campaign, Anonymous has released a list of over 1,000 Twitter accounts that it claims IS supporters use to spread propaganda. The group has also vowed to launch its "biggest operation ever" against IS, promising to use its cyber skills to "unite humanity" and warning terrorists to "expect massive cyber attacks."

However, experts caution that Anonymous' efforts may not be as effective as they seem. Joe Gallop, head of the hacktivism intelligence practice at iSight Partners, notes that IS supporters can easily re-establish new accounts and that the group's use of encrypted communication methods makes it difficult to track their online activities. Moreover, Michael Smith, principal and chief operating officer at Kronos Advisory, warns that uncoordinated efforts by groups like Anonymous can disrupt law enforcement investigations and inadvertently benefit the enemy.

Meanwhile, the Islamic State has responded to Anonymous' declaration of war with a statement on its Telegram channel, urging its supporters to take precautions against cyber attacks. The group advised its followers to avoid suspicious links, change their locations using workaround technology, and rename their email addresses to avoid detection.

In a separate development, the French authorities conducted a raid in the Saint-Denis neighborhood of Paris, where three suspected terrorists were believed to be hiding. The operation resulted in gunfire exchanges between the suspects and police, but the outcome remains unclear.

Ghost Security Group, a subgroup of hacktivists that has worked closely with Anonymous, has also been actively tracking IS online activities. The group has handed over valuable information to government officials, which has helped disrupt planned attacks in Tunisia. Smith, who serves as an intermediary between Ghost Security Group and the government, believes that outside support is crucial in combating IS' online presence, but emphasizes the need for coordination with law enforcement agencies to ensure that efforts do not inadvertently aid the enemy.

As the conflict between Anonymous and IS continues to unfold, the global community watches with bated breath, wondering whether the hacktivist group's efforts will ultimately make a meaningful difference in the fight against terrorism.

reference: – Anonymous is following through with its promise to target ISIS in a cyber war after the Paris terror attacks. Operating under the hashtags #opISIS and #opParis, the group has begun leaking the personal details of alleged ISIS recruiters, reports the Independent. Another list, spotted by Tech Insider, includes more than 80 names, though the site notes that Anonymous has wrongly identified extremists before. The group is also working to take down ISIS-affiliated websites using denial-of-service attacks to overload servers. ISIS, however, seems unperturbed. "All they can do is hacking (sic) Twitter accounts, emails etc.," the terrorist group said in a statement Monday, calling Anonymous a bunch of "idiots." Still, ISIS shared tips with supporters about how to avoid cyberattacks, including altering email addresses, changing a computer's shared location, and ignoring suspicious links, per Newsweek. While Anonymous' attacks could hinder communications between ISIS supporters, analysts tell the Christian Science Monitor that they're unlikely to do much good. "What they are doing is more of simple harassment," one says. "In the end it is not going to help law enforcement to prevent an actual physical attack like what happened in Paris." (ISIS has a 24-hour help desk for jihadis having tech trouble.)

final_summary:
Deep beneath the icy expanse of Greenland's highest point, Summit, lies a 2.7-million-year-old secret - a remnant of a lush, prehistoric landscape that once flourished on the island. Researchers led by Paul Bierman, a geomorphologist at the University of Vermont, made the groundbreaking discovery, published in the journal Science on April 17. 

According to the study, pollen and plant DNA found in the seafloor off Greenland's coast suggest that the island was once home to a tundra and patchy forests, eerily reminiscent of the current Arctic landscape. The team's findings indicate that this verdant landscape remained exposed for 200,,000 to 1 million years before being entombed under the weight of ice.

Bierman and his team unearthed the ancient soil, which dates back 2.7 million years, from beneath the Greenland Ice Sheet during the GISP2 deep-drilling project in 1993. By employing a geochemical technique called beryllium-10 dating, the researchers were able to determine the age of the soil and unlock its secrets. The high levels of beryllium-10 in the soil, comparable to those found in long-lived Arctic landscapes, confirmed that the soil had indeed been exposed to the elements for millions of years.

The discovery has significant implications for our understanding of the Earth's climate history. The fact that the Greenland Ice Sheet has persisted for so long, even during periods of extreme warming, suggests that it has never fully melted, despite experiencing a natural warming event 130,000 years ago that rivaled the predicted effects of this century's climate change.

Moreover, the study reveals that the ice sheet's persistence is due to its unique characteristics. Unlike warm-based glaciers, which erode their underlying terrain, the Greenland Ice Sheet is frozen to its bedrock, allowing it to deform like glass or warm plastic above a stationary base. This phenomenon, known as a cold-based glacier, has protected the ancient landscape beneath, preserving it for millennia.

The researchers' findings have sparked renewed interest in the stability of the Greenland ice sheet, particularly in relation to its potential impact on sea-level rise caused by human-induced climate change. As Bierman noted, "human activity may spell the end of it." The preservation of the ancient landscape beneath the ice sheet serves as a poignant reminder of the delicate balance between the Earth's climate systems and the consequences of human actions.

The discovery of the 2.7-million-year-old soil has far-reaching implications for the field of glaciology and our understanding of the Earth's climate history. As Bierman aptly put it, "the bottom of an ice sheet can preserve some really old landscapes." The team's research has opened doors to new avenues of inquiry, including the search for ancient landscapes in Antarctica, where the researchers hope to uncover even more secrets about the planet's distant past.

reference: – The soil under the highest point of the Greenland Ice Sheet, scientists have learned, is 2.7 million years old, LiveScience reports. In other words, the silt buried under thousands of feet of ice "has been preserved from beyond the dawn of humankind," says Paul Bierman, who determined the soil's age through a geochemical dating process. The findings also suggest Greenland wasn't always as frosty as it is today: Instead, it was a tundra for between 200,000 and one million years before it was encased in ice. It was once home to bits of forest, the scientists found using offshore plant DNA. In other words, as the International Business Times has it, "Greenland really was green." The Greenland Ice Sheet that's there now, however, has been remarkably durable: The findings imply that it made it through a strong period of warming some 130,000 years ago. "It's unlikely the ice sheet has disappeared for significant periods of time in the last three million years, at least at this one pinprick on this big island," Bierman says. "Now human activity may spell the end of it." (What else lies under Greenland's ice? A canyon that dwarfs the Grand Canyon, an aquifer holding 100 billion tons of water, and two huge lakes.)

final_summary:
Tamerlan Tsarnaev, the older brother involved in the Boston Marathon bombings, lay unclaimed at the Boston medical examiner's office for nearly two weeks following his death on April 19. On Saturday, his body was eventually taken into custody by a funeral home, Graham Putnam & Mahoney Funeral Parlors in Worcester, Massachusetts, after his uncle, Ruslan Tsarni, a Maryland resident, arranged for the transfer. Tsarni had previously condemned the alleged bombings, calling the perpetrators "losers."

Two weeks ago, a frantic manhunt for the surviving bombing suspect drew the attention of gun rights advocates at their convention in Houston, with National Rifle Association Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre stating that residents were "imprisoned" in their homes with no means to protect themselves while police searched for the younger Tsarnaev. He questioned how many Bostonians wished they had a gun during that time.

Tsarnaev's death certificate, obtained by CNN, lists the cause of death as "gunshot wounds of torso and extremities" and "blunt trauma to head and torso," with the manner of death classified as homicide. The document indicates that Tsarnaev was shot by police and run over by a car during a confrontation with law enforcement. His body was initially ferried to a funeral home 30 miles away from Boston, near the Rhode Island state line.

The funeral home, owned by Peter Stefan, is working to arrange a burial for Tsarnaev, despite facing resistance from several cemeteries in Massachusetts and neighboring states. Stefan expressed frustration with the situation, stating that everyone deserves to be buried, regardless of the circumstances surrounding their death. He noted that his funeral home has experience handling Muslim services, including washing and preparing the body for burial.

However, several cemeteries have declined to accept Tsarnaev's body due to concerns about potential backlash from the public. Stefan acknowledged these fears, saying that some people might be upset if Tsarnaev were buried near their loved ones. To address these concerns, Stefan's funeral home will maintain a police detail outside the premises throughout the weekend.

In related news, Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the younger brother, is currently being held at a federal Bureau of Prisons medical facility in Devens, Massachusetts, where he faces charges related to the use of a weapon of mass destruction. He is receiving treatment for gunshot wounds sustained during a shootout with police.

Meanwhile, an independent government review is underway to examine whether intelligence and law enforcement agencies could have done more to investigate Tamerlan Tsarnaev prior to the attack. President Barack Obama defended U.S. efforts, stating that preventing attacks by self-radicalized individuals is extremely challenging.

On Friday, a law enforcement source revealed that Tsarnaev's official cause of death would be listed as gunshot wounds and blunt trauma. As the process of arranging a burial continues, Tsarnaev's widow, Katherine Russell, remains under scrutiny, with her lawyer claiming she had no knowledge of her husband's plans to bomb the Boston Marathon.

Three friends of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev have been accused of covering for the bombing suspects, including Azamat Tazhayakov and Dias Kadyrbayev, who removed items from Tsarnaev's dorm room after the bombings. The FBI is examining a laptop seized from Kadyrbayev, which is believed to contain evidence related to the bombings.

In other developments, the Boston area continues to recover from the aftermath of the bombings, with 12 people still hospitalized with injuries.

final_summary:
Today, an 80-year-old woman took her own life at the historic Berkeley City Club in Berkeley, California, resulting in the evacuation of hotel guests and residents. She used a toxic chemical, sodium azide, in her room on the third floor of the six-story landmark building. 

According to Berkeley Police Sergeant Joe Okies, the incident occurred around 1:56 p.m. when emergency services received a call reporting "a person down." Upon arrival, responders discovered a potential suicide involving hazardous materials, prompting a coordinated effort to safely remove the substance. Berkeley Police, Berkeley Fire, and the University of California Police Department collaborated in the response.

Berkeley Police Officer Ethell Wilson stated that hazardous materials crews and bomb squad technicians were dispatched to the scene, and Durant Avenue between Telegraph and Shattuck avenues was temporarily closed to ensure public safety. Although the exact number of evacuees was unknown, there were no reported injuries as of Tuesday evening.

As the investigation progressed, the Berkeley City Club issued a statement assuring its members that none were involved or harmed in the incident. However, the relationship between the deceased woman and the club remained unclear. Berkeley Police assumed control of the situation after the initial response led to a determination that the presence of the hazardous material necessitated a law enforcement response.

Deputy Fire Chief Avery Webb explained that the presence of the hazardous material required a law enforcement response. The Berkeley City Club, designed by Julia Morgan, is a hotel and private social club featuring amenities such as an indoor swimming pool, restaurant, hair salon, and meeting spaces.

Berkeleyside, a local news outlet, provided updates on the developing story, which was also covered by the Oakland Tribune. Journalist Kristin J. Bender reported on the incident, highlighting the use of sodium azide, a colorless salt commonly found in car airbag systems, which becomes acutely toxic when mixed with water.

final_summary:
Under the tumultuous tenure of the Trump administration in the United States, relations between the White House and the press corps covering it have reached an all-time low. Amidst this backdrop, comedian Samantha Bee aims to inject some levity and address the strained dynamics with a unique solution – a gala dinner dubbed "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner." Scheduled to take place on April 29 at the Willard Hotel in Washington, the event coincides with the annual dinner that the White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA) traditionally holds, which has been shrouded in uncertainty due to the contentious relationship between the Trump administration and the press.

Bee, host of the TBS satirical-comedy program "Full Frontal," revealed that the concept emerged in November after the presidential election, when she and her team pondered whether the traditional dinner would continue under a Trump presidency. They decided to create an alternative event, allowing them to tackle topics and roast the President in a manner they deemed fitting. Bee emphasized that their initiative is not intended to supplant the WHCA dinner but rather serve as a backup plan in case the traditional event does not materialize.

The WHCA dinner, which has been held since 1983, typically features high-profile celebrities and comedians, such as Seth Meyers and Stephen Colbert, who have used these platforms to critique the administration. However, the current atmosphere surrounding the event has become increasingly awkward, with journalists and celebrities struggling to navigate the delicate balance between humor and offense. In contrast, Bee's "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner" promises to provide a space for comedians to tackle the Trump administration without restraint.

During a conference call with reporters on Monday, Bee stated that the event will also aim to raise awareness about the challenges faced by journalists, with all proceeds going to the Committee to Protect Journalists. Bee expressed her concerns about the press being under attack under the Trump administration, highlighting the need for a platform where unsung journalists can be recognized and celebrated. The evening will feature a diverse range of performers, although specific details remain undisclosed.

In recent years, late-night hosts have adapted to changing viewer habits by incorporating innovative approaches to engage their audiences. Shows like "Full Frontal" have experimented with social media, publishing humorous content on platforms like Medium and sharing behind-the-scenes insights via Facebook Live. Bee's decision to organize the alternative dinner reflects this trend, as she seeks to connect with her audience and maintain relevance in a rapidly evolving media landscape.

As the date of the WHCA dinner draws near, the fate of the traditional event remains uncertain. Trump's strained relationship with the press has led to speculation about whether he will participate or cancel the event altogether. Meanwhile, Bee's "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner" stands as a testament to the resilience of satire and the importance of free speech in the face of adversity. By providing a platform for comedians to tackle the Trump administration, Bee hopes to inject some much-needed levity into the polarized discourse and remind Americans of the value of a robust and independent press.

reference: – If Donald Trump chooses to roast journalists, celebrities, and politicians at the annual White House Correspondents' Dinner in Washington, DC, this year, it'll be with the knowledge that he's being brutally mocked not too far away. Samantha Bee of TBS' Full Frontal says she'll host celebrities, journalists, and comedians at a Trump roast at the Willard Hotel in Washington on April 29. Bee tells the New York Times that the goal of the event, dubbed "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner," is not to "supersede" the official dinner scheduled for the same night, but simply to "ensure that we get to properly roast the president." She promises "plenty of surprises," per Variety. "If you're not careful you just might learn something. Specifically, you'll learn how screwed we'd be without a free press," she adds. It's not yet clear when or how the event will be broadcast, or even who will attend, but Bee says proceeds will go to the Committee to Protect Journalists. Whether the official Correspondents' dinner will happen is also unclear given Trump's tumultuous relationship with the press, his history with the dinner, and "his oft-demonstrated thin skin," reports the Times. The Independent notes the dinner has been an annual occurrence since 1983.

final_summary:
In a significant breakthrough, Italian authorities claim to have cracked a cryptic code hidden within a mysterious document linked to the notorious 'Ndrangheta mafia organization. Discovered during an investigation into a high-profile murder in Rome in January last year, the coded text is believed to be part of an ancient oath-taking ritual used by the 'Ndrangheta to initiate new members into their ranks. According to Renato Cortese, head of the police rapid response team in Rome, the document reveals the formula that aspiring members must recite to become part of the 'Ndrangheta, specifically within the "San Luca" clan.

The coded message, inscribed on three sheets of note paper alongside firearms and ammunition, was deciphered after two dedicated police officers with a passion for puzzles spent weeks unraveling its secrets. Described as a mix of Arabic, Cyrillic, and Chinese-type script, the translation is said to be rich in symbolism, shedding light on the mystical aura surrounding the induction of new recruits into the 'Ndrangheta fold. This enigmatic aspect of the mafia's inner workings has long fascinated investigators, who see it as a testament to the organization's enduring commitment to tradition and secrecy.

As one of Europe's most feared and influential crime syndicates, the 'Ndrangheta has expanded its reach beyond Italy's borders, establishing connections with Colombian drug cartels and solidifying its position as the continent's largest cocaine smuggler. With its roots firmly planted in Calabria, a region in southern Italy, the 'Ndrangheta has surpassed Sicily's Cosa Nostra as Italy's most powerful mafia, according to recent assessments. The discovery of this coded document serves as a poignant reminder of the 'Ndrangheta's continued influence and adaptability, even as it navigates the complexities of modern organized crime.

According to BBC Rome correspondent Alan Johnston, the finding highlights the 'Ndrangheta's ability to balance its involvement in large-scale business ventures with adherence to traditional practices, including the use of arcane codes and rituals. As the Italian authorities continue to grapple with the ever-evolving nature of organized crime, the deciphered text offers a rare glimpse into the inner workings of the 'Ndrangheta, underscoring the need for sustained efforts to combat this formidable force.

reference: – "For the baptism, five people are needed—no more and no less," reads the coded instructions. But this "baptism" isn't the usual sort: It's for new members of the 'Ndrangheta crime syndicate in Italy, reports the Telegraph. Police discovered the instructions during a murder investigation, and two officers who like to solve puzzles got busy deciphering the text, which resembles ancient Egyptian and Greek. The big break came when they figured out the letter C, and the rest fell into place. It's oh-so-dramatic stuff: The initiation must take place in a "sacred, sacrosanct, and secure" location, and the newbie must cut his skin with a knife in order to swear a blood oath—"an eternal allegiance to the honored society.” The BBC reports that the 'Ndrangheta is thought to be the most powerful mafia in Italy, with a specialty in cocaine smuggling. "Finding such a document shows that even if they are projected towards big businesses and are a criminal group with a global presence, they still use archaic systems," says a police official in Rome. (The syndicate apparently isn't a fan of the new pope.)

final_summary:
Jamie Merrett, a tetraplegic man from Devizes in Wiltshire, was left severely brain-damaged after a nurse accidentally switched off his life-supporting ventilator in January 2009. The incident occurred a few days after Merrett had installed a bedside camera in his home due to concerns over the quality of care he was receiving. The footage, obtained by the BBC through a confidential report by Wiltshire social services, showed nurse Violetta Aylward turning off the ventilator, leaving Merrett without oxygen for 21 minutes before paramedics were finally able to restart the machine.

Merrett, who had been paralyzed from the neck down since a road accident in 2002, was able to communicate using a voice-activated computer and had a relatively high level of cognitive function prior to the incident. However, his sister Karren Reynolds reported that his condition significantly deteriorated after the accident, with his level of understanding dropping to that of a young child.

A confidential report by Wiltshire social services revealed that the agency supplying Aylward, Ambition 24Hours, was aware of its responsibility to provide nurses trained in the use of ventilators but lacked adequate systems to verify the qualifications of its staff. The Nursing and Midwifery Council suspended Aylward pending an investigation into the incident, stating that a nurse should only work within their level of competence and possess the necessary skills to perform their duties.

The NHS Wiltshire Primary Care Trust acknowledged the incident and stated that it had taken steps to prevent similar incidents in the future. However, the trust's ability to comment further was limited due to ongoing litigation. Merrett's family is considering taking legal action against those responsible for the incident, with his solicitor describing it as one of the most egregious cases of nursing negligence he had encountered.

final_summary:
On Monday, during a Rose Garden press conference at the White House, US President Donald Trump held a gathering to discuss the recently agreed-upon trade deal between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico. In this setting, Trump called upon Cecilia Vega, a reporter from ABC News, to pose a question. However, before she could even receive the microphone, Trump made a comment suggesting Vega was surprised or shocked that he had chosen her to speak next. Vega promptly responded, stating she wasn't in a state of shock, to which Trump retorted, "That's okay. I know you're not thanking. You never do."

The initial transcript of the event, released by the White House several hours later, contained a slight alteration of Trump's words, changing it to "I know you're not thanking. You never do" instead of his original statement. On Tuesday, the White House issued a correction to the transcript, accurately reflecting Trump's actual comments.

Vega continued to pursue her line of questioning, asking about Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh, but Trump initially declined to address the topic, limiting himself to discussing trade-related matters. He eventually allowed Vega to reask her question regarding Kavanaugh. The exchange garnered significant attention online, with Vega expressing her perspective on the situation, stating that a news conference allows reporters to ask any question they deem relevant under the First Amendment.

reference: – President Trump made headlines Monday when he delivered a slam to ABC News journalist Cecilia Vega. Then the White House kept the story alive with a controversial official transcript—one that toned down Trump's remark—before fixing it after an outcry. You can watch the original back and forth for yourself in this video. Trump selects Vega to ask the first question at a news conference, then jokes that she is shocked at that. "I'm not, thank you, Mr. President," Vega responded. Trump, apparently mishearing her, then says, "I know you're not thinking. You never do." However, Politico reports that the first version of the White House transcript, out later Monday, quotes Trump as saying: "I know you're not thanking. You never do." The transcript was spotted by Voice of America journalist Steve Herman, who tweeted the pertinent section and said he wasn't buying it. "I was sitting just behind her in the Rose Garden and we all clearly heard him say: "I know you're not thinking. You never do.” As critics accused the White House of whitewashing the exchange, a revised transcript appeared Tuesday morning, reflecting that Trump did indeed say "thinking," reports NBC News.

final_summary:
Windi C. Thomas, a 44-year-old resident of Erie, has pleaded guilty to third-degree murder in connection with the fatal attack on her boyfriend, 44-year-old Keeno Butler, at their shared east Erie residence in March. As part of her plea agreement with prosecutors, Thomas has agreed to a recommended sentence of 18 to 36 years in state prison, which cuts two to four years from the maximum sentence of 20 to 40 years for third-degree murder. 

The incident occurred on March 18 when Thomas allegedly attacked Butler, causing blunt force trauma to his neck and thoracic area, leading to his death. According to investigators, Thomas claimed that she used a folding knife to cut Butler's hand and struck him in the head with a table leg during a confrontation. Furthermore, she admitted to placing her head on the couch, resulting in her body compressing Butler's head, exacerbating the injuries he sustained.

Erie County Judge William R. Cunningham accepted Thomas' plea on Monday, just before 9 a.m., allowing Assistant Erie County District Attorney Jeremy Lightner to drop charges of aggravated assault, reckless endangerment, and possession of the instrument of a crime. Thomas' court-appointed lawyer, Mark Del Duca, described the outcome as a "good result" considering the circumstances of the case, stating that it was a fair compromise for both parties involved.

However, the victim's sister, Sandra Butler, expressed disappointment with the proposed sentence, advocating for a harsher punishment. She believes that Thomas should receive a minimum of 40 years in prison, emphasizing that her brother's life was invaluable. Butler's family continues to grapple with the loss, with Sandra revealing that her mother still calls out for her deceased brother every night.

Thomas will remain incarcerated in the Erie County Prison without bail until her scheduled sentencing hearing on December 21.

reference: – A 300-pound woman in Pennsylvania has pleaded guilty to murdering her boyfriend by stabbing him, hitting in the head with a table leg, and finally crushing the 120-pound man with her own weight. Windi Thomas, 44, pleaded guilty to third-degree murder in the March 18 death of Keeno Butler in exchange for a recommended sentence of 18 to 36 years in state prison, the Erie Times-News reports. Police say Thomas admitted crushing Butler after a fight at their Erie residence. She told them she positioned herself so that some of her weight would be on his head. The death was ruled a homicide "caused by respiratory insufficiency secondary to blunt force trauma to the neck and thoracic compression, exacerbated by blunt force trauma to the head," court papers state. Sandra Butler, the victim's sister, says Thomas should have gotten life. "My mother, she calls for him every night," she says. Thomas, who had been scheduled to go on trial next week, will be sentenced Dec. 21, the AP reports. (A Fitbit led to this man's arrest after he claimed an obese intruder killed his wife.)

final_summary:
Comedy Central confirmed on Tuesday that Jon Stewart, the host of "The Daily Show," will be leaving the anchor's chair after nearly 17 years. Stewart, 52, announced his decision to depart during the show's taping earlier that day, stating that he felt it was time for someone else to have the opportunity he had. The exact date of his departure remains uncertain, with Stewart indicating it may occur in September, December, or July, depending on the final details being worked out. 

Later this year, Stewart will conclude his tenure as host of "The Daily Show," a position he has held since January 1999, succeeding Craig Kilborn. Under his leadership, the show has won 20 prime-time Emmys and become a cultural touchstone for millions of fans. During his tenure, Stewart emerged as a leading political satirist and one of the toughest interviewers in the news media, earning him the nickname "the most trusted man in America."

Throughout his career, Stewart has directed several films, including "Rosewater," a drama about the detention of Iranian-Canadian journalist Maziar Bahari during Iran's 2009 election protests. He has also appeared in various other projects, such as "The Larry Sanders Show" and "Crossfire," where he famously sparred with host Tucker Carlson on the alleged malign impact of cable news on American political discourse. Getty Images captured many iconic moments of Stewart's career, showcasing his wit and charisma.

As a result of Stewart's departure, Comedy Central released a statement praising his contributions to the network, citing his unique voice and vision, which have made "The Daily Show" an unparalleled platform for political comedy. Michele Ganeless, the president of Comedy Central, stated that Stewart has been at the heart of the network, championing and nurturing the best talent in the industry, and that the show will endure for years to come. Every day, Stewart has inspired audiences with his sharp commentary and incisive interviews.

Stewart's exit marks the latest change in the Comedy Central late-night lineup, following the departure of Stephen Colbert, who hosted "The Colbert Report" before taking over for David Letterman on CBS. Another notable departure was John Oliver, who left to host the breakout hit "Last Week Tonight" on HBO. Despite these changes, Comedy Central remains confident in the show's enduring legacy, with Ganeless emphasizing that "The Daily Show" will continue to thrive beyond Stewart's departure. Last year, the network saw significant shifts in its programming, but it remains committed to producing innovative and engaging content.

In a heartfelt address to his audience, Stewart expressed gratitude for the experience and acknowledged that the show didn't deserve a restless host. He praised the talented individuals he has worked with, describing them as "creative, collaborative, and kind." Stewart's emotional farewell marked the end of an era for "The Daily Show," but his impact on American politics and satire will undoubtedly be remembered for years to come.

reference: – Jon Stewart is retiring from the Daily Show later this year. He reportedly revealed the big news during the taping of tonight's episode, reports the AV Club. In a statement to Politico, a Comedy Central exec confirms that Stewart will step down at a still-unspecified date after more than 15 years. "Through his unique voice and vision, the Daily Show has become a cultural touchstone for millions of fans and an unparalleled platform for political comedy that will endure for years to come," says network chief Michele Ganeless, which seems to suggest that the show will go on with a new host. This is one of those rare cases when a network's official statement is right in line with what most others are writing: "To a younger generation of viewers who stopped watching the news, his late-night 'fake' news show—which skewered politicians and the media—made him a trusted messenger to millions," writes Emily Yahr at the Washington Post. Business Insider: The show is a "cultural force." NPR: A "hugely influential figure in American politics." New York Times: A "nightly home for sharp-edged political satire and up-to-the-moment commentary on the news." (Stewart, of course, is a movie director now, too.)

final_summary:
Yesterday, conservative provocateur James O'Keefe faced backlash for a proposed prank targeting CNN reporter Abbie Boudreau. According to O'Keefe, the plan involved luring Boudreau onto a boat, where he would pretend to seduce her in a satirical attempt to counter her reporting methods. However, the details of the plan, as outlined in a document obtained by CNN, raised concerns about the potential for coercion and exploitation.

O'Keefe claimed that the document was a draft and not intended to be carried out as written, citing that he often receives numerous ideas and schemes, some of which he may approve of in principle but not in their entirety. He stated that he liked the basic concept of turning the tables on Boudreau but was repulsed by the explicit language and symbolism used in the document, which was drafted by his associate Ben Wetmore, someone he referred to as his mentor.

However, evidence suggests that O'Keefe's account may be disputed. His accomplice, Izzy Santa, believed that the plan involved creeping Boudreau out, implying that the intention was not to engage in a harmless satirical stunt but rather to create an uncomfortable situation. Furthermore, the document described a scenario involving a boat with strawberries and champagne, which seems inconsistent with O'Keefe's claim that the plan was to be conducted in a conservative setting.

Rush Limbaugh, a prominent conservative figure, had previously targeted Michelle Obama, the wife of President Barack Obama, with sexist attacks, labeling her "Michelle, My Butt." Meanwhile, conservative media outlets, including Andrew Breitbart's BigGovernment.com, published cartoons mocking the First Lady's anti-obesity campaign, with one cartoon depicting her demanding "lard."

In response to these criticisms, O'Keefe defended himself, accusing the liberal media of being biased and claiming that his actions were misunderstood. He pointed to his past successes, including the downfall of ACORN, a community organization that had faced criticism for its handling of government funds. However, his defense was met with skepticism, particularly given his association with Wetmore, who had a history of creating provocative content.

This controversy unfolded amidst a broader debate about government intervention in American life, with the Obama administration's efforts to address obesity and other health issues facing opposition from conservative critics. On the same day, conservative commentator Simon Maloy criticized O'Keefe's actions, highlighting the potential risks and consequences of his proposed prank.

reference: – Andrew Breitbart, fresh off the news of Shirley Sherrod's lawsuit against him, has gotten himself embroiled in another controversy. His Big Government website lashed out at Michelle Obama's anti-obesity campaign ... by basically calling her fat. A cartoon strip on the website shows an overweight Obama eating a huge pile of hamburgers while talking to her big-eared husband about the campaign, Salon reports. The punch line—"Shut up and pass the bacon!"—was originally "Shut up and pass the lard!" Media Matters notes. Click to see the cartoon.

final_summary:
On Wednesday, August 29, 2012, Hurricane Isaac made landfall in Louisiana, causing widespread destruction and chaos along the Gulf Coast. The storm brought with it powerful winds of up to 80 mph and heavy rainfall, resulting in flooding and power outages across the region. According to CNN, over 817,000 customers were left without power, with the majority of outages occurring in Louisiana. 

In New Orleans, Mayor Mitch Landrieu imposed a dusk-to-dawn curfew, warning residents to stay indoors due to the hazardous conditions. The city's levees, strengthened after Hurricane Katrina in 2005, held up relatively well, but the surrounding areas experienced severe flooding. In Plaquemines Parish, a rural levee was overtopped, causing water to pour into the area and stranding dozens of residents in attics and on rooftops. Parish President Billy Nungesser estimated that as many as 800 homes may have suffered significant water damage in the parish alone.

According to CNN affiliate WWL, rescue efforts were underway, with the National Guard launching a large-scale rescue operation to reach stranded residents. However, the slow-moving storm posed challenges for rescue teams, with Isaac's winds and rain continuing to hamper efforts. The National Weather Service issued a tornado warning for southern Mississippi, including the cities of Long Beach and Gulfport, as the storm crawled inland.

Governor Bobby Jindal activated the National Guard to assist with rescue efforts and declared a state of emergency. The Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) deployed four UH-60 Blackhawk helicopters and two SH-60 Seahawk helicopters to aid in search and rescue operations. The National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) warned of potentially catastrophic flooding, with totals reaching up to 20 inches in some areas.

As the storm continued to move inland, the National Hurricane Center (NHC) reported that Isaac's maximum sustained winds had decreased to 60 mph. The storm's slow pace allowed it to dump heavy rainfall on the region, causing flash flooding and landslides. The NHC warned of isolated tornadoes and rough surf along the coast, advising residents to exercise caution.

In Mississippi, the storm caused significant disruptions, with over 4,000 homes without power and 2,132 evacuees housed in 31 shelters across the state. According to CNN affiliate WALA, the city of Pensacola, Florida, reported that it was back to normal operations, with city hall, libraries, and garbage collection functioning as usual.

As the storm continued to wreak havoc, officials scrambled to respond to the crisis. The Louisiana Attorney General reminded sex offenders that evacuating due to the storm did not exempt them from notifying law enforcement of their whereabouts. The National Weather Service issued a flash flood warning for several Louisiana cities, including New Orleans, and a tornado warning for Hancock and Harrison counties in Mississippi.

The storm's slow pace and large size worsened its effects, making it seem like a stronger storm than its Category 1 classification suggested. The NHC warned of potentially catastrophic flooding, with totals reaching up to 20 inches in some areas. As the storm continued to move inland, the NHC reported that Isaac's maximum sustained winds had decreased to 60 mph.

In the aftermath of the storm, officials began assessing the damage and coordinating relief efforts. The National Guard continued to assist with rescue operations, and utility companies worked to restore power to affected areas. The storm's impact was felt across the region, with thousands of residents displaced and countless homes damaged or destroyed. As the sun rose on Wednesday, August 29, 2012, the full extent of the devastation became clear, leaving a trail of destruction in its wake.

reference: – Today is the 7th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, and for residents of Plaquemines Parish, it's deja vu all over again, according to the parish president. Billy Nungesser tells NPR that the parish, located some 95 miles from New Orleans, has already seen damage that rivals, and could even surpass, that heaped upon it by Katrina. With the levee overrun, Nungesser says parts of the parish that had escaped unscathed in previous hurricanes now sit under five feet of water. "I don't know who's calling this a Category 1, but this is no Category 1," Nungesser said. "My house has more damage than it did during Katrina." More on the situation in Plaquemines, and beyond: The parish's levees range from 8.5 feet to 12 feet in height. The Army Corps of Engineers estimates the storm surge hit 12.5 feet, but have yet to confirm a breach. This implies the levees, which are not part of the federal levee system, failed, reports the Times-Picayune. While the director of Plaquemines Parish's emergency preparedness says winds may have hit 110mph, CNN reports that Isaac has weakened a bit since, with maximum sustained winds of 75mph (the hurricane threshold is 74mph). As of about 10:30am ET, Isaac's center was located about 50 miles south-southwest of New Orleans. The AP reports that 500,000 are without power in the areas around New Orleans. In Southern Mississippi, a tornado warning has been issued.

final_summary:
This Spring, Apple has made official its announcement regarding the availability of the highly anticipated white iPhone 4, which will hit stores tomorrow. According to Philip Schiller, Apple's senior vice president of Worldwide Product Marketing, "The white iPhone 4 has finally arrived and it's beautiful." The device will be available in 16- and 32-gigabyte versions for $199 and $299, respectively, with a two-year wireless contract through Apple, Verizon, AT&T, and other authorized resellers. 

In related news, Apple has addressed concerns over the collection and storage of location data on iOS devices. In a statement, the company clarified that it does not track the location of customers' iPhones and has never done so. However, Apple acknowledged that users may have been confused due to a lack of understanding about how the device provides fast and accurate location information. The company identified several bugs that contributed to the confusion, including the storage of a crowd-sourced database of Wi-Fi hotspots and cell towers surrounding the device's location, which can be hundreds of miles away. This database was maintained in Cupertino, where Apple is headquartered.

Apple maintains that it cannot identify the source of this data, and that the stored cache is not the actual location of the device. To address these concerns, the company plans to release a free iOS software update within the next few weeks that will reduce the size of the database, encrypt the data, and delete it when Location Services are turned off by the user. Additionally, another bug that caused the iPhone to continue updating its location data even after Location Services were disabled will be fixed. These developments come amidst a class-action lawsuit filed against Apple, alleging invasion of privacy and computer fraud, as well as investigations by French, German, Italian, and South Korean regulators into the alleged location collection feature on Apple devices. Apple's response to these concerns includes a comprehensive Q&A session addressing the issue, which can be found online. Earlier this week, the company faced increased scrutiny following the filing of the class-action lawsuit. Rumors had previously suggested that the white iPhone 4 would be released by the end of April, with some reports indicating a launch date of April 27.

Earlier this week, the company faced increased scrutiny following the filing of the class-action lawsuit. Rumors had previously suggested that the white iPhone 4 would be released by the end of April, with some reports indicating a launch date of April 27.

reference: – Apple finally responded to the accusations that it was tracking iPhone users’ locations today, saying it “has never done so and has no plans to ever do so.” In a statement, which you can read on TechCrunch, the company explained that it was actually keeping a log not of the phone’s location, but of the location of WiFi hotspots and cell towers, “some of which may be located more than one hundred miles away from your iPhone.” The data is used to more quickly triangulate users’ position when they use location-based services, and is sent to Apple anonymously. But the company did admit to some “bugs” in the software—like that it keeps collecting data even after you’ve turned Location Services off, and that it stored years of data, instead of the week's worth it needs—and promised an iOS update to fix them. And, just to add some positive news to the cycle, it also announced that the rumored white iPhone will be available tomorrow, Time reports.

final_summary:
On June 8, U.S. celebrity chef Anthony Bourdain, 61, was found dead in his hotel room in Kaysersberg, France, where he had been working on an upcoming episode of his CNN series "Parts Unknown" with his close friend, French-born chef Eric Ripert. Bourdain had been open about his struggles with substance abuse in the past, including a history of heroin and cocaine addiction, but had maintained that he had remained sober since entering rehab. A recent toxicology report revealed that at the time of his death, Bourdain had no narcotics in his system, although a small amount of a non-narcotic medicine was detected in a therapeutic dose, along with a trace of alcohol.

Bourdain's death was confirmed as a suicide by hanging, with a French judicial official stating that there was no evidence of foul play or violence involved. This conclusion was further supported by a medical expert who found no signs of violence on Bourdain's body. The investigation also found that Bourdain had been in good health prior to his death, with no underlying medical conditions that could have contributed to his decision to take his own life.

In the days following his death, tributes poured in from around the world, with friends, colleagues, and fans expressing their shock and sadness at the loss of the charismatic and adventurous chef. Among those paying tribute was his girlfriend, Asia Argento, an Italian actress who had been dating Bourdain for over a year. Argento, 42, shared a heartfelt message on Instagram, saying that Bourdain had given all of himself in everything he did and that his bravery and generosity had inspired countless people.

Bourdain's relationship with Argento had been well-documented, particularly in the wake of her high-profile allegations against film producer Harvey Weinstein, whom she accused of rape. Bourdain had been a vocal supporter of Argento, praising her courage and fearlessness in speaking out against Weinstein and others in Hollywood who had enabled his behavior. Just before his death, Bourdain had expressed his pride in Argento's speech at the Cannes Film Festival, calling it an "incredibly powerful moment."

As news of Bourdain's death spread, fans and fellow celebrities took to social media to pay their respects, sharing memories and stories of the chef's adventures and exploits. In Paris, where Bourdain had been staying, fans gathered outside the hotel where he had been staying, leaving flowers and messages of condolence. 

In the aftermath of his death, CNN released a statement mourning the loss of its beloved colleague and friend. As the news sank in, fans and admirers began to reflect on the impact that Bourdain had had on their lives, from his irreverent travel shows to his bestselling books and cookbooks.

Two weeks after his death, on June 22, Argento posted a poignant message on Instagram, sharing a photo of herself and Bourdain smiling together in front of a serene blue body of water. "Two weeks without you," she wrote, her words a testament to the enduring bond between the couple and the profound impact that Bourdain's death had left on those who loved him.

final_summary:
A 2-year-old black Labrador named Bear has proven himself to be a valuable asset in the fight against cybercrime. Trained to sniff out electronic data devices, Bear is one of only five dogs in the nation with this specialized skillset, and his impressive track record includes playing a pivotal role in the high-profile arrest of former Subway pitchman Jared Fogle on child-porn charges. 

Fogle, whose real name is Jared Fogle, pleaded guilty to possessing X-rated images of minors and paying to have sex with teenage girls after a thorough investigation by authorities in Indiana. During the search of Fogle's residence in July, several weeks before he agreed to plead guilty, Bear's keen sense of smell proved invaluable, detecting a thumb drive that human investigators had missed. This remarkable feat showcased the effectiveness of Bear's training, which involves getting him accustomed to the scent of electronic devices and rewarding him with treats when he indicates their presence.

Bear's handler, Todd Jordan, has been working with the talented canine since he was a puppy and has witnessed firsthand its exceptional abilities. Jordan demonstrated Bear's skills by hiding various electronic devices in a room and instructing him to "seek" them out; within mere minutes, Bear located a memory card, a flash drive, and an iPad. This impressive display of Bear's capabilities has earned him recognition from prosecutors, including Steven DeBrota, who led the Fogle case and praised Bear as "a key part of the team."

Bear's contributions extend beyond the Fogle case, having participated in four other investigations, including the recent arrest of Olympics gymnastics coach Marvin Sharp. Just this week, he took part in another investigation. His services will soon be utilized by the Seattle Police Department, where he will aid in investigating internet crimes. As Bear prepares to relocate to Seattle, his current owner, Todd Jordan, expressed mixed emotions, acknowledging that while it will be difficult to part with the dog, he takes pride in knowing that Bear's work will continue to make a significant impact.

Jordan's expertise extends beyond Bear, as he trains additional canines to join the ranks of electronic-sniffing dogs. With Bear set to begin his new chapter in Seattle, his legacy as a pioneering canine asset continues to grow, serving as a testament to the innovative ways in which technology and animal intelligence can be combined to combat crime.

reference: – There are only five dogs in America trained to sniff out electronic devices, and one of them played a major role in Jared Fogle's downfall. Bear, a 2-year-old black Labrador, was able to sniff out a thumb drive that human investigators missed during a search of the former Subway spokesman's home last month, reports NBC News, which describes Bear as a "porn-sniffing dog." He can detect tiny memory cards easily and was trained with methods associated with "any other K-9 training, like with the narcotics or explosives or anything," his trainer tells WISH-TV. "You get the dog used to the odor and reward them as they indicate on it." The lead prosecutor on the Fogle case tells NBC that Bear is a "key part of the team," though he doubted the pooch's abilities at first. "I thought I was being punked, but it does work," he says. The evidence, along with that gathered by an FBI informant, helped secure Fogle's guilty plea on child-porn and underage sex charges. Bear has been a busy dog: NBC reports that he also played a role in the arrest of Olympic gymnastics coach Marvin Sharp. Bear's trainer, a deputy fire chief, sells dogs to law enforcement agencies when their training is completed, and Bear will soon be on his way to Seattle, where the police department contacted the trainer after hearing about the Fogle case. (Fogle's charitable foundation appears to have been a sham.)

final_summary:
Paige Warner, an 11-year-old girl from Roseville in Northern California, tragically lost her life after suffering a severe allergic reaction during a family Thanksgiving trip to Hawaii. Paige, who had pre-existing food allergies and sensitivities to environmental conditions, had been excitedly looking forward to visiting the islands with her family, including her parents, Karin Carpenter and Hank, and her younger sister, Violet.

On Thanksgiving Day, the family arrived in Honolulu and spent the evening at a friend's home for a holiday meal in the Hawaii Kai neighborhood. Despite being unable to eat much of the food due to her allergies, Paige seemed fine initially, enjoying time with her new friends and getting into the pool with Violet. However, shortly after diving into the water, Paige began experiencing severe symptoms, complaining of a burning sensation in her nose and difficulty breathing. Her mother, Karin Carpenter, quickly realized something was seriously wrong and called 911.

An EMT who was a guest at the gathering administered Benadryl to Paige, but she struggled to take it. The ambulance arrived within seven minutes, and paramedics administered epinephrine and Benadryl en route to Kapiolani Children's Hospital. Despite efforts to revive her, Paige suffered cardiac arrest and required over 20 minutes of CPR before her heart was restarted. She was placed on life support and remained in a coma, undergoing various medical treatments, including a cooling blanket to reduce her body temperature.

As Paige's condition worsened, her family remained by her side, praying, playing music, and reading messages from friends to her. Her biological father, Josh, flew in to visit her, although he was unable to stay until the end due to feelings of guilt for neglecting his responsibilities towards Paige in the past. An MRI on December 1 revealed significant damage to Paige's brain, with the exception of the brain stem and the area responsible for memory. Tragically, further testing confirmed that Paige's brain had suffered irreparable damage, leading to her passing at 9:25 a.m. 

KTXL reported that Paige's lungs closed up and her heart stopped, and she received CPR for about an hour before her heart started again. The family was supported by a GoFundMe page set up to raise funds for them during this difficult time, which had brought in thousands of dollars. As the family mourns Paige's loss, her younger sister Violet has found solace in helping her parents cope, insisting on decorating the house for Christmas, a tradition Paige loved dearly.
