reference: – On Thanksgiving Day, Paige Warner and her family flew to Honolulu for the 11-year-old's first trip to Hawaii. She "was so excited," recounts mom Karin Carpenter, who explains that after a holiday dinner of white rice and soy sauce—Paige had "extensive" food allergies—her daughter got into the pool with her sister. Fewer than 15 minutes later, she was out of the pool, complaining of a stinging nose. Her condition quickly snowballed into something much more serious, involving difficulty breathing, and she was taken to the ER. On Dec. 4, after a week in a coma, the girl from Roseville, Calif., was taken off life support; it's still unclear what triggered the reaction, reports KTLA. As Carpenter writes on a GoFundMe page, she speculates about possible contributing factors: the plane, entering an environment full of allergens that were new to her, the area's large feral cat population, pesticide runoff in the pool from an adjacent golf course, or a bronchial spasm caused by the pool's temperature. The family is "devastated," but sister Violet is bringing healing, insisting that the family hang its Christmas decorations because Paige "loves Christmas." The GoFundMe campaign has raised more than $30,000 to date. The most heart-tugging line on the page, from Carpenter's description of the events immediately after Paige got out of the pool: "She looked into my eyes and screamed, and said, 'Mom, I'm dying. I know I am because I can see it in your eyes.'" (A teen's allergic reaction recently caused her to "burn from the inside out.")

final_summary:
In a shocking turn of events, a disturbing case of child abuse came to light in West Palm Beach, Florida, leaving a trail of horror and tragedy in its wake. Jorge Barahona, a father of four adopted children, including 10-year-old twins Victor and Nubia, was found alongside his son in a red pickup truck on Interstate 95, with Nubia's lifeless body covered in acid and wrapped in a plastic bag in the back of the vehicle.

According to eyewitnesses, Barahona had attempted to take his own life by pouring gasoline over his head, but changed his mind when he realized his son, Victor, was beside him. Victor, who was also severely injured, was rushed to the hospital with severe burns to his abdomen, upper thighs, and buttocks, as well as prior injuries including a fractured clavicle, a fractured arm, scarring to his buttocks and lower abdomen, and ligature marks on both wrists.

A probable-cause affidavit filed by the West Palm Beach Police Department revealed that Barahona had initially claimed he was distraught over Nubia's death and had given Victor a handful of sleeping pills, telling him to take them so he could sleep. However, police say his story doesn't add up, as Victor was covered in a different chemical, whose composition had yet to be determined, and there was no gasoline on his body.

Carmen Barahona, Jorge's wife, was present in court on Monday, where a child welfare judge ordered that the remaining two children in the home, a 7-year-old girl and an 11-year-old boy, be placed in foster care. The Florida Department of Children and Families had previously investigated the couple on three separate occasions, although the cases were closed without finding any evidence of abuse or neglect. However, a recent investigation was launched after a teacher reported concerns about the twins being bound by their hands and feet and made to stand in the bathtub.

On the same day, a Florida Department of Transportation employee discovered the truck parked on the side of I-95, with Victor inside, suffering from respiratory distress and soaked in an unknown chemical. Barahona was found on the ground outside the truck, and a hazardous materials crew was called in to clean up the scene.

Alessandra, a 6-year-old relative who had stayed with the Barahonas after school, had previously witnessed the twins being locked in a bathroom with a shower curtain taped to the walls and being forced to stand in garbage bins. She had confided in a therapist, prompting a short-lived child welfare investigation. The case highlights the need for vigilance in protecting vulnerable children from abuse and ensuring that those responsible are held accountable for their actions.

Mark Riordan, a DCF spokesman, confirmed that all evidence indicated Nubia was deceased, while Victor was clinging to life in the burn unit at Jackson Memorial Hospital in Miami.

history.draft:
In a shocking turn of events, a disturbing case of child abuse came to light in West Palm Beach, Florida, leaving a trail of horror and tragedy in its wake. Jorge Barahona, a father of four adopted children, including 10-year-old twins Victor and Nubia, was found alongside his son in a red pickup truck on Interstate 95, with Nubia's lifeless body covered in acid and wrapped in a plastic bag in the back of the vehicle.

According to eyewitnesses, Barahona had attempted to take his own life by pouring gasoline over his head, but changed his mind when he realized his son, Victor, was beside him. Victor, who was also severely injured, was rushed to the hospital with severe burns to his abdomen, upper thighs, and buttocks, as well as prior injuries including a fractured clavicle, a fractured arm, scarring to his buttocks and lower abdomen, and ligature marks on both wrists.

A probable-cause affidavit filed by the West Palm Beach Police Department revealed that Barahona had initially claimed he was distraught over Nubia's death and had given Victor a handful of sleeping pills, telling him to take them so he could sleep. However, police say his story doesn't add up, as Victor was covered in a different chemical, whose composition had yet to be determined, and there was no gasoline on his body.

Carmen Barahona, Jorge's wife, was present in court on Monday, where a child welfare judge ordered that the remaining two children in the home, a 7-year-old girl and an 11-year-old boy, be placed in foster care. The Florida Department of Children and Families had previously investigated the couple on three separate occasions, although the cases were closed without finding any evidence of abuse or neglect. However, a recent investigation was launched after a teacher reported concerns about the twins being bound by their hands and feet and made to stand in the bathtub.

On the same day, a Florida Department of Transportation employee discovered the truck parked on the side of I-95, with Victor inside, suffering from respiratory distress and soaked in an unknown chemical. Barahona was found on the ground outside the truck, and a hazardous materials crew was called in to clean up the scene.

Alessandra, a 6-year-old relative who had stayed with the Barahonas after school, had previously witnessed the twins being locked in a bathroom with a shower curtain taped to the walls and being forced to stand in garbage bins. She had confided in a therapist, prompting a short-lived child welfare investigation. The case highlights the need for vigilance in protecting vulnerable children from abuse and ensuring that those responsible are held accountable for their actions.

Mark Riordan, a DCF spokesman, confirmed that all evidence indicated Nubia was deceased, while Victor was clinging to life in the burn unit at Jackson Memorial Hospital in Miami.

reference: – It's one of the more horrific allegations of a child abuse to come along for some time: Florida police found the corpse of a 10-year-old girl, covered in acid, in the back of her adopted father's truck. Her twin brother was in the front seat, nearly overcome by toxic fumes, and is recovering in intensive care from previous injuries including a broken arm and collar bone. Their adopted father, 53-year-old Jorge Barahona, was found outside the truck and has been charged with aggravated child abuse for now, reports CNN. The story coming together suggests the twins were adopted by Jorge and his wife and subjected to systemic abuse, including being bound by their hands and feet and forced to stay in the bathroom, reports the Miami Herald. State authorities had two previous reports of something amiss, including one on Feb. 10, but took no action against the parents. A heart-wrenching detail from the Herald: An investigation finally began when the couple's biological granddaughter told her therapist she wanted to break open her piggy bank to help the twins escape. Click for more.

final_summary:
In a high-profile terrorism case unfolding in Chicago, defense attorneys for Adel Daoud, a 19-year-old man accused of attempting to detonate a car bomb outside a downtown bar, are questioning the government's motives behind withholding information about the origins of the evidence used to charge their client. The case has drawn attention to the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA), specifically Section 702, a provision that allows the government to intercept foreign targets' emails and phone calls without an individual warrant. 

According to a motion filed by Daoud's lawyers, Thomas Durkin and Joshua Herman, the government is deliberately avoiding disclosure of whether the evidence used to charge Daoud was obtained through Section 702, fearing a potential constitutional challenge to the law. The lawyers argue that if the government acknowledges using evidence derived from Section 702, Daoud would have standing to challenge the law's constitutionality. However, the government has refused to confirm or deny the use of Section 702, instead claiming that the evidence was obtained through a traditional FISA court order.

U.S. Senator Dianne Feinstein, chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, previously cited the alleged plot to bomb a downtown Chicago bar as an example of the effectiveness of the FISA Amendments Act, which was passed in 2008. However, the government is now refusing to say whether this specific case involved the use of Section 702. Daoud's lawyers contend that the government is being disingenuous, pointing out that Senator Feinstein's statement suggests that the law was indeed used in the case.

Earlier this month, the government refused to say whether the evidence was obtained under Section 702, instead stating that it was acquired pursuant to a traditional FISA court order. A traditional order requires the government to go to a FISA judge and show probable cause that the target is an agent of a foreign power. Daoud's lawyers say in their pleading that the government is being disingenuous. "We believe it is clear that the evidence...came from Section 702," Durkin said in an interview. "Either Senator Feinstein's information was correct in December 2012, or she was given wrong information. The government has never disputed what she said."

The FISA Amendments Act, which governs the government's surveillance program, has come under intense scrutiny since the revelations about the scope of the program. Critics argue that Section 702's breadth and lack of individual warrants raise significant constitutional concerns. The law targets non-U.S. persons reasonably believed to be located outside the U.S., without requiring a warrant for interception.

Daoud's case began when he came under FBI scrutiny after posting online messages about killing Americans. FBI agents posing as terrorists exchanged messages with him, helping him plan an attack. He was arrested in September after attempting to detonate a bomb outside a Chicago bar in Hillside, Illinois. Daoud has pleaded not guilty and is set to go on trial in February.

Lawyers for Daoud argue that the government is trying to have it both ways – boasting about the effectiveness of the surveillance program when convenient, while hiding behind state secrets when it suits their interests. The case highlights the tension between national security concerns and individual rights, as well as the need for transparency and accountability in the use of surveillance powers.

reference: – Adel Daoud is a Chicago 19-year-old accused by the feds of trying to detonate a bomb outside a Chicago bar in 2012. His case, however, could serve as the first constitutional challenge to the government's sweeping surveillance techniques, reports the Washington Post. In a court filing yesterday, attorneys for the terror suspect demanded to know whether federal authorities used their expanded powers under a 2008 amendment to the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act to nab him. “Whenever it is good for the government to brag about its success, it speaks loudly and publicly,” they wrote, as per the Chicago Tribune. "When a criminal defendant’s constitutional rights are at stake, however, it quickly and unequivocally clams up under the guise of State Secrets.” Federal prosecutors say they're not obligated to say whether they used the law, and a judge will have the final say, reports WLS-TV. So did they use it? Here's a hint: Last year, when the Senate was debating whether to renew the 2008 law—it's formally known as the FISA Amendments Act—Dianne Feinstein made her case in favor by asserting that it had helped stop "a plot to bomb a downtown Chicago bar." If the judge rules that the government must acknowledge it used the FAA, Daoud's attorney's would then be able to challenge its constitutionality.

final_summary:
On Wednesday morning, Mayor Dario Nardella of Florence announced an initiative aimed at discouraging tourists from eating and drinking on the steps of the city's churches. As of lunchtime, city authorities began implementing the plan, which involves pouring water over the church steps to make them unwelcoming for picnickers. According to Nardelli, the goal is to redirect tourists to nearby cafes and restaurants, which offer outdoor seating and tables, rather than treating the churches as dining areas. 

The first locations targeted by the initiative were the areas surrounding the Basilica of Santa Croce and the Santo Spirito church. Nardella stated that the city would assess the effectiveness of the measure before deciding whether to extend it to other areas, including pavements. However, some locals expressed concerns that the move may deter tourists from visiting the city or be perceived as elitist, particularly towards those who prefer to eat takeaway food instead of dining at expensive restaurants.

Social media users criticized the decision, with some labeling it as "orrorre" (as tweeted by @Stefy_1987 on May 31, 2017), implying that it targets those who cannot afford to dine at upscale establishments. Others pointed out that the water-based solution might not be effective in the sweltering heat of the Italian summer, suggesting that picnickers might not mind getting wet.

In response to these criticisms, Nardella clarified that the city would not employ powerful water cannons to target tourists but rather focus on cleaning the church steps and pavements during lunchtime. He emphasized that the city values tourists but aims to address the disrespect shown towards its cultural heritage, citing instances of visitors leaving trash behind and disregarding the historical significance of the sites.

This initiative is part of a broader effort to tackle the issues associated with mass tourism in Florence, which attracts approximately 12 million visitors each year. Under Nardella's leadership since 2014, the city has implemented various measures to mitigate the negative impacts of tourism. These efforts include regulating restaurants in the historic center to ensure they serve at least 70 percent locally sourced products and blocking applications for fast-food chains like McDonald's to operate in sensitive areas, such as the Piazza del Duomo.

The city's actions follow similar initiatives taken by other Italian cities, including Rome, which has imposed fines on individuals caught eating on the Spanish Steps. In contrast, Florence opted for a more gentle approach, choosing to use water to deter visitors from congregating on church steps. The city's goal is to strike a balance between welcoming tourists and preserving its rich cultural heritage.

reference: – The mayor of Florence says some tourists have no "decorum" these days—something he hopes to fix with a water hose. Mayor Dario Nardella says a small number of the Italian city's 12 million annual tourists—particularly day-trippers from cruise ships—have taken to "camping out" on church steps at lunchtime. They "sit down on church steps, eat their food, and leave rubbish strewn on them," with no respect for "our cultural heritage," Nardella tells the Guardian. He says city officials thought about handing out fines to such tourists, but they decided instead to get creative. The plan: Use water hoses to soak church steps. That way, if tourists sit down, "they'll get wet," Nardella said in announcing the strategy Wednesday. Shortly after the announcement, the steps of both the Basilica of Santa Croce and the Chiesa di Santo Spirito were doused. In the future, this will be a daily occurrence, reports the Telegraph. "Instead of imposing fines, we thought this measure was more elegant," Nardella tells the Guardian, adding other areas might eventually get a wash as well. While some social media users joked that a free shower might be nice in the heat of summer, others have accused city officials of being "elitist" in targeting tourists who choose not to eat in Florence's restaurants, per the Local. Nardella counters that the city has plenty of outdoor seating for tourists. Churches, however, are no place for a picnic, he says. (Barcelona has an issue with its tourists, too.)

final_summary:
A groundbreaking study published in the esteemed journal Psychological Science has shed new light on the intricate relationship between human emotions and color perception. Led by researchers C. A. Thorstenson and A. D. Pazda, in collaboration with A. J. Elliot, a psychologist at the University of Rochester in New York, the study aimed to investigate whether emotions can influence color perception. According to Thorstenson, "Color is such an important part of our experience," and the research sought to uncover the underlying mechanisms behind the common observation that people's moods can alter their perception of colors.

As reported in TIME Health, the study's findings suggest that our emotional state can significantly impact how we perceive the world around us. This notion is echoed by the famous phrase "not in Kansas anymore," referring to the classic tale of Dorothy's journey through the Land of Oz, where her surroundings transformed dramatically after a tornado swept her away. Similarly, the study reveals that our emotional state can change the way we perceive colors, making the world seem duller or brighter depending on our mood.

In the study, participants were randomly assigned to watch either a sad film clip, an amusing clip, or a neutral screen saver. The sad clip featured a poignant scene from Disney's The Lion King, where Mufasa's death evokes intense sorrow, accompanied by the haunting music of Elton John. The amusing clip consisted of a stand-up comedy routine, while the neutral condition involved watching a blank desktop screensaver. After viewing the clips, participants were tasked with identifying desaturated colors, specifically red, yellow, green, and blue patches that had been muted to gray.

The results revealed a striking correlation between emotional state and color perception. Participants who watched the sad clip struggled to distinguish between shades along the blue-yellow color axis, whereas those who watched the amusing or neutral clips performed accurately. Notably, the impairment in color perception was specific to the blue-yellow axis, suggesting that sadness may have a unique impact on the processing of these colors. This finding is consistent with previous research indicating that dopamine, a neurotransmitter involved in mood regulation, plays a crucial role in color perception.

Thorstenson's study builds upon the idea that emotions can influence low-level visual processes, which are essential for higher-order vision. The researchers propose that the connection between emotions and color perception may be rooted in the brain's neural pathways, where dopamine transmission is critical for processing visual information. The study's findings have implications for our understanding of how emotions shape our subjective experience of the world.

Interestingly, the study's results echo the notion that our perception of reality is not solely determined by objective stimuli but is also influenced by our internal emotional state. This concept, known as the "top-down" effect, suggests that higher-level cognition can modulate perception, rather than merely reflecting it. While some researchers argue that the evidence for top-down effects is still limited, Thorstenson's study contributes to the growing body of research exploring the complex interplay between emotions, cognition, and perception.

The study's lead author, C. A. Thorstenson, emphasized that the findings have broader implications for our understanding of the human experience. He stated, "Our results show that mood and emotion can affect how we see the world around us." The study's conclusions are supported by previous research demonstrating that exposure to certain colors can influence cognitive performance, facial attractiveness, and food intake. As Thorstenson noted, "Data are beginning to emerge supporting the conventional wisdom that people's emotions influence how colorful the world looks to them."

The study's significance extends beyond the realm of basic science, offering insights into the intricate relationships between emotions, cognition, and perception. By shedding light on the complex interactions between the brain's neural networks, the study provides a deeper understanding of how our experiences shape our perception of reality. As Thorstenson aptly put it, "How we feel can really influence how we see the world around us."

reference: – Got the blues? You probably aren't seeing blues clearly. That's the takeaway from a new study that finds how a person views the color blue may actually depend on mood, reports Medical Daily. Not all colors were affected in the same way. Researchers at the University of Rochester asked 129 college students to watch either a stand-up comedy clip or the scene from The Lion King in which Mufasa dies, then recorded the participants' moods, per the Washington Post. Next, participants took a color accuracy test in which they had to look at 48 color patches that had been desaturated to look nearly gray, then identify whether each was red, yellow, green, or blue. People who were feeling down in the dumps performed worse than those who were amused. In other words, the data supports "the conventional wisdom that people's emotions influence how colorful the world looks to them," the researchers say. However, the sad group performed poorly only when quizzed about colors on what researchers call the blue-yellow axis. They could identify colors on the red-green axis just as well as others—perhaps due to the evolutionary need to see red as a sign of anger, reports Time. The results are "a reminder that our experience of the world is not as immediate and objective as we’d like to believe," observes the Post. Researchers suggest a sad mood messes up the ability of the neuron transmitter dopamine to process information about blue-yellow colors. (This woman sees 100 times more colors than you.)

final_summary:
On October 19, 2018, CNN's Chief White House Correspondent Jim Acosta tweeted from President Donald Trump's rally in Missoula, Montana, expressing frustration over the loud music being played by the Trump campaign, which made it nearly impossible for him to conduct live TV reports. He wondered if the high volume posed a security concern for local law enforcement, who may also be unable to hear due to the excessive noise.

Justin Caporale, a Republican consultant and former aide to First Lady Melania Trump, responded to Acosta's tweet with a mocking remark, "Dear Diary…", implying that Acosta was whining like a child writing in a diary. This seemingly innocuous jab sparked a heated exchange between the two, with Acosta sending Caporale a direct message containing a profanity-laced insult, "F**k you." However, unbeknownst to Acosta, Caporale had shared their conversation with Daily Caller reporter Peter Hasson, who obtained screenshots of the exchange and publicly revealed them on social media.

The incident quickly went viral, with many conservatives taking to Twitter to mock Acosta's outburst. In response to the backlash, Acosta issued an apology to Caporale, claiming he had mistaken him for an "old friend from the campaign days" and expressed regret for offending him. The apology came hours after the initial exchange, and it remains unclear why Acosta chose to block Caporale shortly after sending the insulting message. Representatives for CNN declined to comment on the matter, while Caporale also refused to discuss the incident further.

reference: – One of the reporters who tangles publicly with President Trump the most is himself in a bit of hot water over a tweet. Jim Acosta of CNN has apologized after tweeting "f--- you" (except he spelled it out) to a critic, reports Mediaite. Acosta also maintains that he mistook the person as an old friend, but not everyone is buying that. It all started Thursday night when Acosta tweeted a complaint that the Trump campaign turned up the music so loudly at a Trump rally that it was difficult to work—the implication being that it was deliberate. At that point, a former aide for Melania Trump, Justin Caporale, mocked Acosta with a tweet reading, "Dear Diary ..." That's when Acosta sent his controversial response—he did so in a private direct message, but Caporale then made it public. Acosta also blocked Caporale. As online criticism grew, and Caporale demanded an apology, Acosta responded: "Hey buddy I thought you were an old friend from the campaign days. I’m so sorry. Hope I didn’t offend you. Have a good night and take care." As the Wrap notes, the apology seems a bit odd given that Acosta also blocked Caporale, raising the question of why he would block a friend. Neither Acosta nor CNN has commented further. (Acosta also once tangled memorably with White House aide Stephen Miller.)

final_summary:
Schlitterbahn Waterparks and Resorts, a Texas-based company operating a waterpark in Kansas City, Kansas, announced on Tuesday that the 168-foot-tall Verrückt waterslide, where a 10-year-old boy named Caleb Schwab died in August, would be permanently decommissioned and removed from the tower once the investigation into the tragedy is concluded and permission is granted by the court. Caleb, the son of Kansas state Representative Scott Schwab, was decapitated while riding the slide, which was billed as the world's tallest waterslide, featuring multi-person rafts making a 17-story drop at speeds of up to 70 mph.

The incident occurred on August 7, and two other riders in the same raft suffered facial injuries. Since then, the ride has been closed, and Schlitterbahn has cooperated with investigators and the victims' families and attorneys, stating that the safety of their staff and guests is their top priority. In a statement, the company expressed their heartbreak over the tragedy and acknowledged that they had never experienced such a devastating event in their 50 years of providing an environment for families and friends to gather.

According to the statement, once the investigation is completed, Verrückt will be closed permanently, and the slide will be removed from the tower. A Schlitterbahn spokesperson told 41 Action News that the slide "will never run again – not at any park, anywhere," and that the park is prepared to dismantle the slide and conveyor belt as soon as permitted by the court.

The investigation into the tragedy is ongoing, with a homicide detective still examining the ride's components, including rafts, netting, and metal frames. The case remains open, although criminal charges are unlikely. The Schwab family's attorney, Michael C. Rader, stated that he has been working with Schlitterbahn and their attorneys to permanently decommission the slide and ensure its eventual demolition, pending the conclusion of all investigations and potential litigation.

As the investigation continues, the possibility of a lawsuit or settlement remains uncertain. In Kansas, there are limits on personal injury and wrongful death cases, with caps of $300,000 for non-economic damages and $250,000 for wrongful death cases. If the case proceeds to court, it may be moved to Texas due to Schlitterbahn being a Texas-based company. An independent investigation is also underway, involving attorneys and experts for the Schwab family and the two female passengers who were injured in the incident.

In related developments, states' regulations of waterslides and other amusement rides have faced scrutiny following Caleb's death. Kansas has relatively light regulation of amusement park rides, allowing private inspectors to conduct annual checks instead of requiring state inspections. A document released by the state Department of Labor revealed that all of Schlitterbahn's rides passed private inspections in June.

reference: – The 10-year-old son of a Kansas lawmaker died on the world's tallest water slide this summer, and that ride is now closed for good. Caleb Schwab, the son of state Rep. Scott Schwab, suffered a "fatal neck injury" Aug. 7 on the 168-foot Verruckt slide at the Schlitterbahn Kansas City water park; the slide has been closed as investigators look into Caleb's death, the Kansas City Star reports. Once the probe is done and the park gets the legal go-ahead, the slide will be "decommissioned," with the ride permanently shuttered and the slide taken down from its tower, per a Schlitterbahn statement released Tuesday. "In our opinion, it is the only proper course of action following this tragedy," the park says, adding it remains "heartbroken" over Caleb's death. It's not clear whether the Schwab family will sue or settle with the park, or if criminal charges will be filed, though the Kansas City PD tells KSHB charges aren't likely. In Kansas, permanent amusement park rides have to undergo yearly inspections, but they can be done by private firms rather than the state, the AP reports. In Schlitterbahn's case, all of its rides received a thumbs-up in June after they received private inspections. But some people who had ridden the slide, operational since 2014, spoke out after Caleb's death and noted they'd had uneasy experiences on the ride, including shoulder harnesses coming loose and rafts flying into the air when they shouldn't. The park notes in its release that it will eventually announce what attraction will be built in Verruckt's place.

final_summary:
For decades, physics students have been taught that it would take approximately 42 minutes to fall through the center of the Earth, a concept often referred to as the "gravity tunnel problem." However, a recent study published in the March issue of the American Journal of Physics, a publication of the American Association of Physics Teachers, challenges this widely-held notion, suggesting that the actual time may be shorter – around 38 minutes and 11 seconds.

The study was conducted by Alexander Klotz, a graduate student in physics at McGill University in Montreal, Canada. According to Klotz, the discrepancy arises from the assumption that Earth has a uniform density, which is far from the truth. Using seismic data, Klotz created a more realistic model of Earth's internal structure, revealing a less dense crust and mantle, and a more dense core. By applying this new model to the gravity tunnel problem, Klotz found that the time it would take to fall through the center of the Earth is significantly reduced.

David Jackson, a physicist at Dickinson College in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, and editor of the American Journal of Physics, praised Klotz's work, noting that it offers a fresh perspective on a classic problem. "This is the kind of paper we love," Jackson said. "It not only adds more detail about the structure of Earth but also explains why you can replace one overly simple assumption with another equally crude one and still get a much more accurate answer."

Klotz's findings suggest that the traditional method of calculating the time it would take to fall through the Earth assumes a constant force of gravity, which is not representative of the actual conditions. In reality, the force of gravity varies depending on the distance from the center of the Earth. By accounting for this variation, Klotz found that the time it would take to fall through the center of the Earth is closer to 38 minutes and 11 seconds.

Interestingly, Klotz discovered that if he assumed a constant gravitational field throughout the duration of the trip, the math showed that it would indeed take approximately 38 minutes to fall through the Earth. This finding highlights the complexity of the problem and the importance of considering the nuances of Earth's internal structure.

Isaac Newton's law of gravity underlies the gravity tunnel problem, which demonstrates both the principles of gravity and cyclical motion. The study's results underscore the need for a more accurate understanding of these fundamental concepts, particularly in light of the complexities of Earth's internal structure. As Jackson noted, "The classic problem needs to stay, but this is a nice addition to the classic problem."
anchors: The American Association Of Physics Teachers

reference: – It's a common hypothetical in physics classrooms: How long would it take to fall through a tunnel from one side of the Earth to the other? The new answer: 38 minutes and 11 seconds, reports Science. The result is courtesy of McGill University graduate student Alexander Klotz, whose calculations take four minutes off the previously accepted answer and appear in the American Journal of Physics. The reason for the shorter time? Unlike previous calculations, Klotz's take into account that the Earth's layers have different densities, and those differences would affect the hypothetical falling man, notes Phys.org. "The way the Earth is structured, the gravity increases slightly as you go deeper towards the dense core, to about 110% of its surface value, before getting weaker as you move through the core, reaching zero at the center,” says Klotz, as quoted in Forbes. (If it's all a little too hypothetical, try physics-inspired pasta instead.)

final_summary:
In a heated debate on Thursday, September 22, 2011, in Orlando, Florida, Republican presidential hopefuls Mitt Romney and Rick Perry clashed over various issues, including immigration, healthcare, and Social Security. The debate, sponsored by Fox News and Google, saw the two front-runners engaging in a series of tense exchanges, with Romney accusing Perry of flip-flopping on several key issues. 

Perry, who has been leading in national opinion polls, faced intense scrutiny over his stance on immigration, particularly his support for a Texas law that provides in-state tuition rates to children of undocumented immigrants. Romney criticized Perry's policy, stating that it creates a "magnet" for illegal immigrants seeking educational benefits. Perry, however, defended his position, arguing that it is unfair to punish children who entered the country through no fault of their own. When Romney pressed him on the issue, Perry shot back, saying, "I don't think they have a heart."

The debate also centered around Social Security, with Romney accusing Perry of advocating for the abolition of the program, citing a passage from Perry's book "Fed Up!" Perry denied the accusation, claiming that he never suggested such a thing. Romney then mocked Perry, saying, "You better find that Rick Perry and get him to stop saying that." Perry retaliated by accusing Romney of flip-flopping on his views on healthcare, pointing out that Romney had initially supported the healthcare reform bill he implemented in Massachusetts, but later removed the language from subsequent editions of his book.

Romney and Perry also exchanged blows over their respective records on gun rights and healthcare. Perry claimed that Romney had changed his stance on gun ownership, while Romney accused Perry of supporting a healthcare plan that would require individuals to purchase insurance or face a fine. The two candidates' aggressive posturing dominated the debate, leaving other contenders, including Herman Cain, Newt Gingrich, and Jon Huntsman, struggling to gain attention.

The debate, which was moderated by Fox News, highlighted the increasingly contentious nature of the Republican primary contest. With Perry leading in the polls and Romney closing the gap, the stakes are high for both candidates. The debate showcased their contrasting styles, with Romney presenting himself as a steady and consistent leader, while Perry came across as more passionate and emotive. Despite the intensity of the exchanges, neither candidate emerged clearly victorious, leaving the outcome of the primary contest still uncertain.

reference: – Republicans got back into debate mode tonight, with Rick Perry and Mitt Romney trading a series of sharp and sometimes sarcastic barbs at one another over Social Security, immigration, and other issues, reports AP. No fewer than nine candidates were on the stage, thanks to the invite extended to former New Mexico Gov. Gary Johnson. (He got perhaps the biggest applause line of the night with this line: "My neighbor's two dogs have created more shovel-ready projects for this country" than Obama.) But Perry and Romney were the main focus, and their back-and-forth on Social Security typified things: Perry said his position on it isn't as radical as his foes say, adding it was "not the first time that Mitt's been wrong on some issues before," notes the New York Times. Romney responded: "There's a Rick Perry out there that's saying the federal government shouldn't be in the pension business. You better find that Rick Perry and get him to stop saying that." Perry then accused Romney of changing views of his Massachusetts health care law in different versions of his book, and later widened the flip-flopping charges to a host of other issues. (Romney used the phrase "nice try" at different times to deflect Perry criticisms.) Immigration also emerged as a main issue, with Romney, Michele Bachmann, and Rick Santorum criticizing the Texas policy of giving in-state tuition benefits to immigrants' kids, notes Politico. Bachmann called such programs "magnets," but Perry said of his critics, "I don't think you have a heart." Catch up with live-blogging and details on the other candidates here.

final_summary:
Dawn Wells, best known for her iconic role as Mary Ann Summers on the classic television show "Gilligan's Island," is facing a dire financial situation after suffering a series of setbacks. In 2008, Wells experienced the devastating effects of the banking crash, resulting in the loss of her life savings. This financial blow was further exacerbated by a life-threatening surgery that left her hospitalized for two months and requiring extensive recovery time. 

In addition to these challenges, Wells was involved in another unexpected accident that necessitated additional hospitalization. Her current predicament has led to mounting medical expenses and penalties, which are accumulating daily. To alleviate these burdens, a GoFundMe campaign was initiated by her friend, Dugg Kirkpatrick, aiming to raise $194,000 to cover her outstanding debts and medical costs. 

As of the latest update, the fundraising effort has garnered nearly $120,000, with contributions pouring in from supporters on social media platforms such as Facebook. Kirkpatrick, a hairstylist who has worked with Wells in the past, has been actively promoting the campaign and advocating for her well-being. He revealed that Wells has been struggling to find suitable housing, having lost her home, and is currently seeking to relocate to a smaller assisted living facility catering to individuals from the film and television industry. final_summary:
Representative Robert E. Andrews (D-N.J.) announced his resignation from Congress on Tuesday, citing a 23-year tenure marked by an unprecedented lack of legislative success. During his time in office, Andrews introduced an astonishing 646 bills, yet only four of them successfully passed the House of Representatives, with none making it to the president's desk due to failing to clear the Senate hurdle. This dismal record makes Andrews the least successful lawmaker of the past two decades, with a batting average that would be considered abysmal in any other context.

However, Andrews disputes this characterization, arguing that the traditional notion of lawmaking – where a bill is introduced, debated, and voted upon – no longer applies on Capitol Hill. According to him, the current system involves massive omnibus bills packed with unrelated provisions, which often succeed despite having little to do with each other. Andrews claims that his true measure of success lies in the numerous ideas he has embedded within these larger bills, which have ultimately become law.

Andrews estimates that approximately 110 of his ideas have been incorporated into other legislation, although this figure remains difficult to verify. His legislative output has been staggering, with no other representative introducing more bills since his arrival in Congress. However, his colleagues have fared better, with Carolyn B. Maloney (D-N.Y.) passing nine bills, Don Young (R-Alaska) securing 76, Charles B. Rangel (D-N.Y.) achieving 38, and Ron Paul (R-Tex.) managing one minor bill.

A closer examination of Andrews' bills reveals a diverse range of topics, including support for Taiwan, commemorative coins honoring Korean immigrants, and duty reductions on various chemicals. While these efforts appear to have failed, Andrews insists that his approach is effective in getting his ideas onto the legislative agenda. He attributes his success to introducing bills that serve as building blocks for future legislation, often referred to as "moving vehicles." These bills frequently gain traction due to impending deadlines or other external factors, allowing Andrews to insert his proposals into the mix.

In a February 11, 2011, file photo, Rep. Robert Andrews (D-N.J.), left, accompanied by Rep. Tim Bishop (D-N.Y.), spoke on Capitol Hill in Washington, D.C. As Andrews departs, his legacy remains a subject of debate, with some questioning whether his unorthodox approach to lawmaking has yielded tangible results. Joshua Tauberer, whose site govtrack.us tracks congressional data, notes that while only one of the companion bills to Andrews' own legislation may become law, the work to get either enacted may have been shared by the two sponsors equally.

reference: – New Jersey Democrat Rob Andrews made a surprise announcement today that he'll be giving up his House seat as of Feb. 18 to join a Philadelphia law firm, reports Philly.com. And while it's big news in Jersey, Andrews isn't exactly a household name nationally. Still, the Washington Post observes that he's got a legislative track record remarkable in its own way: Over 23 years, not one of the 646 pieces of legislation Andrews authored has become law. All the more impressive is that no representative has introduced more bills than him in that span. Sure, most bills fail in Congress, but "by those numbers, Andrews would be America’s least successful lawmaker of the past two decades," writes David Fahrenthold. Andrews, described as a "semi-liberal back-bencher," defends himself by saying that modern lawmaking these days is done through massive pieces of legislation, not through individual bills. By his own reckoning, Andrews estimates that 110 of his ideas have become law in that fashion. If he wants to eliminate the goose egg, however, maybe he can rename a post office in his final two weeks?

final_summary:
On Thursday, a chaotic scene unfolded on the set of "Transformers 4" in Hong Kong, where director Michael Bay was filming. According to multiple accounts, including those from Bay's representatives and the director himself, a man, allegedly under the influence of a narcotic substance, approached the set and attempted to intimidate the crew. This individual, described as a "zombie-like" figure, wielded an air-conditioning unit as a weapon and demanded money from the crew.

The man, reportedly accompanied by two others, had previously accosted several crew members, attempting to extort them for thousands of dollars, specifically mentioning a sum of about $13,000. When confronted by Michael Bay, the situation escalated, and the man lunged at the director with the air-conditioning unit. However, Bay dodged the attack and managed to wrestle the device away, preventing potential harm.

As the situation spiralled out of control, the security team intervened, and the police arrived, eventually arresting four individuals, including the man wielding the air-conditioning unit and two of his companions, for assaulting the officers. The incident occurred on the first day of production in Hong Kong, marking the beginning of a new phase of filming after four and a half months of shooting in Arizona, Utah, Texas, Michigan, Illinois, and Washington State.

In a statement, Gabriela Gutentag, a press representative for Michael Bay, clarified that the director was not injured in the altercation, contrary to initial reports suggesting otherwise. Instead, Bay described the incident in a post on his website, comparing the assailant to a character from Brad Pitt's film "World War Z." The incident even saw the assailant biting into a guard's Nike shoe, which fortunately had an air bubble that popped, saving the toe.

Despite the commotion, filming resumed shortly after, with Bay expressing gratitude towards the Hong Kong authorities and the crew for their professionalism. The incident drew attention from international media outlets, including Reuters and BBC News, which reported on the unusual demands made by the group and the subsequent arrest of the suspects.

reference: – Michael Bay's own rep calls it "bizarre": While filming the latest Transformers flick in China today, the director had what the rep describes as "a bizarre encounter with a man (allegedly under the influence of a narcotic substance) who was wielding an air conditioning unit as a weapon." After accosting a few crew members, he swung the AC unit at Bay's head, the New York Times reports. In true action hero style, Bay ducked and then "wrested the air conditioner from his attacker," the rep's statement continues. The suspect and two companions were arrested. Some reports said Bay was injured, but his rep says no one was hurt. On his website, Bay describes the suspects as "drugged up guys" and "belligerent asses." He says the crew paid local vendors for their inconvenience during filming, but the suspect "wanted four times that amount." After Bay fought him off, security stepped in: "But it took seven big guys to subdue him. It was like a Zombie in Brad Pitt’s movie World War Z—he lifted seven guys up and tried to bite them. He actually bit into one of the guards Nike shoe, insane," Bay writes. "Then it took 15 Hong Kong cops in riot gear to deal with these punks. In all, four guys were arrested for assaulting the officers."

final_summary:
In February 2009, Halle Berry, the Oscar-winning actress, took her ex-partner Gabriel Aubry, the father of their 6-year-old daughter Nahla, to court over a disagreement regarding the child's appearance. According to reports from TMZ and E! News, Berry alleged that Aubry had been straightening and highlighting Nahla's naturally curly hair while in his care, attempting to make her appear less African American. This move sparked a heated argument between the two, with Berry accusing Aubry of trying to erase Nahla's racial identity.

The incident occurred during a custody battle between the two, who have been embroiled in a contentious shared parenting arrangement since their breakup in 2010. Berry, who is of mixed heritage, has previously stated that she considers Nahla to be black and wants her to identify as such. However, Aubry, who is white, has been accused of trying to alter Nahla's appearance to fit his own preferences.

On Monday, a judge intervened, ruling that neither parent can alter Nahla's hair color or texture, and instead ordered that it be allowed to grow back naturally. This decision came after Berry's lawyer, Steve Kolodny, appeared in court on her behalf, while Aubry also attended the hearing.

This is not the only issue that has arisen in the couple's custody battle. Berry has sought to reduce her child-support payments to Aubry from $16,000 a month to less than $4,000, citing concerns that he has been living off the payments without contributing financially. Berry claims that Aubry has been unemployed and has been taking advantage of the system by relying solely on her financial support.

The couple's relationship has been tumultuous, with a history of physical altercations and public disputes. In 2012, Aubry was arrested after a fight with Berry's then-fiancé, Olivier Martinez, and in 2010, Berry and Aubry had been dating for two years before breaking up.

In a 2011 interview with Ebony magazine, Berry expressed her desire for Nahla to choose her own racial identity, stating that she considered herself black and believed in the "one-drop rule," a concept that deems individuals with any amount of black ancestry to be black. Berry acknowledged the challenges of navigating multiple racial identities, saying that she had struggled to balance her black and white heritage throughout her life.

The ongoing custody battle between Berry and Aubry continues to raise questions about the complexities of co-parenting and the importance of respecting each other's differences in a blended family.

final_summary:
Palau, a small Pacific island nation located in Oceania, is shifting its tourism strategy to focus on attracting high-end travelers and reducing the number of visitors. According to President Tommy Remengesau, the government aims to rebrand the country as a luxury destination by introducing a new law that would restrict the construction of hotels to only five-star establishments. This move is part of a broader effort to address concerns about the environmental impact of mass tourism, including damage to coral reefs and disturbance of wildlife. 

Remengesau emphasized the importance of "quality rather than quantity" tourism, citing the need to develop the islands in a way that respects both the natural environment and the local population. To achieve this goal, the government is offering tax breaks and exemptions to high-end investors who can provide their own infrastructure, such as water treatment systems and power backups, and take care of other necessities like building roads. 

The decision to limit tourism growth follows a significant increase in visitor numbers in recent years, with a notable surge in Chinese tour groups. In response, the government halved the number of charter flights from China in 2015, resulting in a decrease in overall visitor numbers but an increase in average spending per tourist. Remengesau believes this approach has confirmed the effectiveness of targeting high-end travelers, who contribute more to the local economy through their higher spending. 

Currently, Palau relies heavily on tourism, accounting for a significant portion of its GDP. However, the influx of visitors has put pressure on the country's resources, leading to concerns about overcrowding, reef damage, and wildlife disturbance. By promoting sustainable and high-end tourism, the government hopes to strike a balance between economic benefits and environmental protection. 

With 500 islands to choose from, Palau offers numerous opportunities for luxury resort development, although the exact locations for the new five-star hotels have yet to be determined. While the initiative shows promise, questions remain about whether the hotels will be built in harmony with the natural environment, preserving the unique beauty of the islands that make Palau a sought-after destination. 

According to iStock images, it's easy to see why tourists flock to Palau, but the government wants to attract fewer people and more money. Palau wants to cut the number of tourists visiting its idyllic shores by making it a more expensive place to visit. The image is courtesy of Norimoto. 

President Remengesau wants to rebrand the Pacific island state as a luxury destination and is proposing a new law that would allow only five-star hotel developments in future, as reported by Radio New Zealand International. He's offering tax breaks for high-end investors if they sort out all their own infrastructure needs, such as building roads and designing water treatment systems for the resorts. 

Palau relies heavily on tourism, but in recent years locals have complained about an influx of visitors causing overcrowding at popular sites, damaging coral reefs and disturbing wildlife. The biggest increase has been among Chinese tour groups, and in 2015 the government announced it was halving the number of charter flights arriving from China. 

President Remengesau says the proposed law focuses on "quality rather than quantity", and he makes clear that Palau wants to attract big spenders, as reported by Marianas Variety. But he says it's also about developing the islands in a way that respects the natural environment and those who call the country home. 

"There's a right way to do things and there's a wrong way to do things," he says, adding that when it comes to development, "the best spots around the world have blended in with the environment." 

Next story: Marshall Islands struggle to restore web. Use #NewsfromElsewhere to stay up-to-date with our reports via Twitter.

Remengesau believes this approach has confirmed the effectiveness of targeting high-end travelers, who contribute more to the local economy through their higher spending.

Currently, Palau relies heavily on tourism, accounting for 85 percent of its GDP. However, the influx of visitors has put pressure on the country's resources, leading to concerns about overcrowding, reef damage, and wildlife disturbance. By promoting sustainable and high-end tourism, the government hopes to strike a balance between economic benefits and environmental protection.

With 500 islands to choose from, Palau offers numerous opportunities for luxury resort development, although the exact locations for the new five-star hotels have yet to be determined. While the initiative shows promise, questions remain about whether the hotels will be built in harmony with the natural environment, preserving the unique beauty of the islands that make Palau a sought-after destination.

history.draft:
Palau, a small Pacific island nation located in Oceania, is shifting its tourism strategy to focus on attracting high-end travelers and reducing the number of visitors. According to President Tommy Remengesau, the government aims to rebrand the country as a luxury destination by introducing a new law that would restrict the construction of hotels to only five-star establishments. This move is part of a broader effort to address concerns about the environmental impact of mass tourism, including damage to coral reefs and disturbance of wildlife. 

Remengesau emphasized the importance of "quality rather than quantity" tourism, citing the need to develop the islands in a way that respects both the natural environment and the local population. To achieve this goal, the government is offering tax breaks and exemptions to high-end investors who can provide their own infrastructure, such as water treatment systems and power backups, and take care of other necessities like building roads. 

The decision to limit tourism growth follows a significant increase in visitor numbers in recent years, with a notable surge in Chinese tour groups. In response, the government halved the number of charter flights from China in 2015, resulting in a decrease in overall visitor numbers but an increase in average spending per tourist. Remengesau believes this approach has confirmed the effectiveness of targeting high-end travelers, who contribute more to the local economy through their higher spending. 

Currently, Palau relies heavily on tourism, accounting for a significant portion of its GDP. However, the influx of visitors has put pressure on the country's resources, leading to concerns about overcrowding, reef damage, and wildlife disturbance. By promoting sustainable and high-end tourism, the government hopes to strike a balance between economic benefits and environmental protection. 

With 500 islands to choose from, Palau offers numerous opportunities for luxury resort development, although the exact locations for the new five-star hotels have yet to be determined. While the initiative shows promise, questions remain about whether the hotels will be built in harmony with the natural environment, preserving the unique beauty of the islands that make Palau a sought-after destination. 

According to iStock images, it's easy to see why tourists flock to Palau, but the government wants to attract fewer people and more money. Palau wants to cut the number of tourists visiting its idyllic shores by making it a more expensive place to visit. The image is courtesy of Norimoto. 

President Remengesau wants to rebrand the Pacific island state as a luxury destination and is proposing a new law that would allow only five-star hotel developments in future, as reported by Radio New Zealand International. He's offering tax breaks for high-end investors if they sort out all their own infrastructure needs, such as building roads and designing water treatment systems for the resorts. 

Palau relies heavily on tourism, but in recent years locals have complained about an influx of visitors causing overcrowding at popular sites, damaging coral reefs and disturbing wildlife. The biggest increase has been among Chinese tour groups, and in 2015 the government announced it was halving the number of charter flights arriving from China. 

President Remengesau says the proposed law focuses on "quality rather than quantity", and he makes clear that Palau wants to attract big spenders, as reported by Marianas Variety. But he says it's also about developing the islands in a way that respects the natural environment and those who call the country home. 

"There's a right way to do things and there's a wrong way to do things," he says, adding that when it comes to development, "the best spots around the world have blended in with the environment."

Remengesau says mass tourism is damaging the environment of Palau, which is made up of around 250 islands, and he wants to pursue a "quality not quantity" strategy. Remengesau says tourism spending actually went up after steps were taken to reduce the number of charter flights from China. (A tourism slogan in Australia is pretty darn explicit.)

final_summary:
According to sources briefed on the investigation, Russian hackers who breached the State Department's network last year used that foothold to gain access to the White House's unclassified computer system, allowing them to view sensitive information about President Obama's schedule. This breach occurred despite the White House's attempts to downplay its severity, with officials initially describing it as affecting only an unclassified system. However, the hackers gained access to real-time, non-public details of the president's schedule, which is considered highly sensitive and prized by foreign intelligence agencies. 

The intrusion began when hackers sent a phishing email from a State Department account, infecting a White House computer with malware. The State Department has been battling its own highly sophisticated cyber intrusion for months, although it remains unclear which parts of its network remain breached. Private security experts had suspected a connection between the State Department and White House breaches, given Russia's history of infiltrating U.S. critical infrastructure, including oil and gas pipelines, wind turbines, and nuclear power plants.

Tuesday, officials confirmed that the White House's unclassified system had been breached, with computers and systems not damaged but some services disrupted as cybersecurity experts worked to limit hacker activity. By late February, the vast majority of systems were back online, according to White House Press Secretary Josh Earnest. However, the breach highlights the risks associated with the White House's reliance on unclassified systems, particularly those handling sensitive information like the president's schedule.

Ben Rhodes, President Obama's Deputy National Security Adviser, acknowledged the potential vulnerability of the unclassified system, stating that the White House uses a separate system for classified information to protect sensitive national security-related items from being obtained by hackers. He emphasized that the White House takes regular actions to prevent vulnerabilities and enhance security on the unclassified system.

The breach is part of a larger pattern of Russian cyberattacks targeting the U.S., with Director of National Intelligence James Clapper warning in January that the Russian cyber threat is more severe than previously assessed. The attack on the State Department's system, which began before Clinton's departure as Secretary of State, has left some officials questioning the safety of her private email server, which critics argue may have been even less secure than the State Department's system.

The White House hack was first reported by CNN on Tuesday, with officials confirming that the breach occurred last year, during a period of heightened tensions between the U.S. and Russia over Ukraine. The hack was described as a demonstration of Russia's willingness to target the heart of American power, with CBS News senior national security analyst Juan Zarate noting that Russia, along with China and other nation-states, possesses advanced cyber tools capable of causing significant harm.

U.S. officials have expressed concern over the breach, with Senator Susan Collins calling the revelations "troubling" and highlighting the need for improved cybersecurity measures. The incident has led to a renewed focus on the importance of protecting sensitive information and preventing future breaches, particularly in light of the ongoing cyber threats posed by Russia and other nations.

reference: – Russian hackers' apparent infiltration of the White House computer network in October wasn't exactly confidence-inspiring, even though it was the unclassified portion of the system that was breached. Now US officials say some of the unclassified information that made its way onto hackers' screens was related to President Obama's itineraries—not classified knowledge, but not exactly public knowledge, notes CNN. The Hill points out that although the press has access to "a general outline" of Obama's daily routine, there are still phone calls and meetings that aren't made public, and that foreign spies would apparently love to know about. Obama's exact location at any given point during the day is also often kept on the down-low, and certain parts of the White House are off-limits to the press, the Hill adds. Besides the White House scheduling and press offices, others who use the hacked system include the general counsel's office and the budget and legislative liaison offices, ABC News reports. The hackers are believed to have accessed the White House network by first hacking State Department files, according to investigators, which leads to the fear that hackers have gotten back into the State Department system. One official tells CNN that the hackers have "owned" the network "for months." "There's always vulnerability," Deputy National Security Adviser Ben Rhodes noted at a press conference, per CBS News. "The fact is that's why we have a classified system because there's less risk in the classified system, and that is secure." (The FBI is going after one Russian hacker in particular.)

final_summary:
President Donald Trump announced on Thursday that the administration would cease making payments to insurers under the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare) program, a move that could significantly destabilize the healthcare market. The decision, which affects an estimated $7 billion in payments this year, comes as the 2018 open enrollment period is set to begin in two weeks. According to the White House, the Department of Health and Human Services has determined that there is no appropriation for the cost-sharing reduction payments, citing guidance from the Justice Department.

This move is seen as the most aggressive attempt yet by Trump to dismantle Obamacare, a law that has expanded private insurance to millions of people through the creation of marketplaces, also known as exchanges, where individuals can purchase plans often subsidized by the government. The law requires that plans offered on the exchanges include essential benefits, such as hospital care, maternity care, and mental health services, and prohibits insurers from denying coverage to people with pre-existing medical conditions.

Healthcare experts warn that the cessation of payments could lead to higher costs for those remaining in ACA plans, as healthier individuals may opt for cheaper, short-term plans that are exempt from ACA requirements. These short-term plans, which do not adhere to the same standards as ACA-compliant plans, could attract large numbers of healthy individuals, leaving behind a riskier pool of patients and driving up premiums.

Trump's decision has sparked widespread criticism from Democrats, with Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi labeling it "spiteful sabotage" that will harm working families and the middle class. New York Attorney General Eric Schneiderman has vowed to sue the administration to prevent the action, while some insurers may also pursue litigation to recover the lost funds.

The move has also raised concerns among Republican lawmakers, with Representative Ileana Ros-Lehtinen expressing her opposition to the decision, stating that it will result in more uninsured individuals in her district. Despite the backlash, the White House maintains that the decision is necessary to address the law's flaws and that Congress should take responsibility for fixing the issue.

However, Trump has hinted that he may reconsider the decision if a bipartisan agreement on healthcare reform is reached. According to sources, he has privately told at least one lawmaker that the payments may continue if a deal is struck. The fate of the subsidies remains uncertain, but the consequences of the administration's decision are clear: a potentially volatile healthcare market and increased financial burdens on those relying on the ACA for coverage.
anchors: The Department Of Health And Human Services

reference: – In a move that analysts say could be a devastating blow to ObamaCare, President Trump is ending one of the health care bill's crucial subsidies. White House press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders confirmed Thursday night that the federal government will stop "cost-sharing reduction" payments to health insurers, which help millions of people afford insurance they get through ObamaCare's exchanges, Politico reports. Sanders called the payments a "bailout of insurance companies" and "another example of how the previous administration abused taxpayer dollars and skirted the law to prop up a broken system." Related coverage: The impact: The Washington Post likens the decision to "throwing a bomb into the marketplaces" created by ObamaCare. Insurers have said this is the strongest step Trump could take to undermine the law, because rising costs would force them to bail without the promise of getting reimbursed. The alternative could be to remain, but to raise premiums to levels most couldn't afford. The New York Times also calls the subsidies "essential" to the Affordable Care Act. Trump's possible offer: "The Democrats ObamaCare is imploding," the president tweeted Friday morning. "Massive subsidy payments to their pet insurance companies has stopped. Dems should call me to fix!" The last part might be the most telling. The Wall Street Journal reports that Trump has told at least one lawmaker that he's open to preserving the payments if a bipartisan deal is struck on health care. All eyes on this: Trump is referring to a deal being negotiated by GOP Sen. Lamar Alexander and Democratic Sen. Patty Murray. Under the broad strokes, the subsidies would be continued, a win for Democrats, but states would get more flexibility to avoid ObamaCare rules, a win for Republicans, per the Journal. Big deadline: Open enrollment for the ObamaCare exchanges begins in less than three weeks, raising pressure for a quick resolution. Background: A federal court declared the subsidies illegal last year, though the decision is being appealed. The subsidies add up to around $7 billion this year, a figure expected to steadily rise in coming years. The attorneys general of California and New York vowed Thursday night that they would challenge the latest move in court, while Nancy Pelosi and Chuck Schumer called it "a spiteful act of vast, pointless sabotage leveled at working families and the middle class in every corner of America." Second move: Earlier Thursday, Trump signed an executive order that's expected to undermine ObamaCare in a different way.

final_summary: On Friday, Disney announced the cast for the next installment in the Star Wars saga, which includes the late Carrie Fisher reprising her iconic role as Princess Leia Organa. Director J.J. Abrams revealed that Fisher will be featured in the film using previously unreleased footage shot for "The Force Awakens," stating that they "desperately loved Carrie Fisher" and were determined not to recast or create a computer-generated character. Abrams expressed gratitude towards Fisher's daughter, Billie Lourd, for giving her support and blessing for the decision to utilize the unseen footage from "Episode VII." This move comes after Disney initially stated in 2016 that there were no plans to digitally recreate Fisher's performance following her passing after suffering a cardiac event while traveling from London to Los Angeles. Despite this initial stance, Abrams explained that they found a way to honor Fisher's legacy and role as Leia in "Episode IX" through the use of the existing footage. The film's production is set to begin soon, with Abrams returning as director alongside co-writer Chris Terrio. Other returning cast members include Daisy Ridley, Adam Driver, John Boyega, Oscar Isaac, Domhnall Gleeson, Kelly Marie Tran, Joonas Suotamo, Mark Hamill, Anthony Daniels, and Billy Dee Williams, along with newcomers Naomi Ackie and Richard E. Grant. The film is part of the Skywalker saga, produced by Lucasfilm, and is expected to conclude the series. John Williams will once again compose the film's score.

final_summary:
In Southern California, a routine patrol in South Pasadena proved to be anything but ordinary for rookie police officer Chris Perez, who had been on the force for only a year. At approximately 6:50 p.m. on Tuesday, Perez received a distressing call about a possible jumper on the York Bridge. As he arrived at the scene, a 12-year-old girl was perched on a 6-inch ledge, teetering on the edge of an 80-foot drop. Perez approached the situation with caution, knowing that a single misstep could prove fatal.

Instead of rushing towards the girl, Perez walked calmly towards her, establishing a connection before initiating a conversation. The girl revealed that she had fled a group home in Los Angeles due to severe emotional distress, citing poor treatment and confinement. Perez employed a gentle and empathetic approach, engaging in a series of conversations with the girl to build trust and establish a rapport.

Through careful questioning, Perez learned that the girl was scared and needed help, and he devised a plan to safely rescue her. He asked permission to move closer and eventually managed to coax her onto the sidewalk, where he offered her water and comforted her as she broke down in tears. When the girl complained of an ankle injury, Perez promptly called for medical assistance.

The girl, who had been living in a group home in Los Angeles, was taken into protective custody for further evaluation. According to police officials, the girl is safe thanks to Perez's calm and professional actions. In an interview with CBS News correspondent Carter Evans, Perez recounted the harrowing experience, highlighting the importance of patience and empathy in crisis situations.

Perez's heroic actions were witnessed by bystanders, including those who initially alerted authorities to the situation. His parents, both of whom are also law enforcement officers, expressed pride in their son's selfless act. While Perez remains humble about his role in saving the girl's life, he acknowledged the significance of his actions and the impact it has had on his family.

This incident serves as a testament to the dedication and compassion of law enforcement officers like Chris Perez, who put themselves in harm's way to protect vulnerable individuals in need. The event was widely reported by local news outlets, including CBS2, where reporter Kristine Lazar highlighted the bravery and quick thinking displayed by Officer Perez during the rescue operation.
